content.content:
1	# The Reckonings Book — Schema & CDC Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft**
4	
5	Owner plugin: `reckoner` · Book name: `reckonings` · Sibling docs:
6	[clockworks.md](clockworks.md), [apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md),
7	[apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md),
8	[apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md).
9	
10	> **⚠️ Forward-looking design.** This document specifies the
11	> Reckoner's evaluation-log book before the Reckoner core itself is
12	> commissioned. The plugin id `reckoner` and the existing
13	> `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` (formerly `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus`;
14	> a narrow queue-observer that emits Lattice pulses for `writ-stuck` /
15	> `writ-failed` / `queue-drained`) have been renamed in a separate
16	> commission so this id can be reused for the new petition-scheduling
17	> Reckoner. Nothing in `packages/plugins/sentinel/` is touched by this
18	> design — see Open Questions for the integration.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Intent
23	
24	The Reckonings book is the Reckoner's **evaluation journal**: one
25	append-only row per petition consideration, persisted to The Stacks as
26	`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')`. Every tick of the Reckoner's
27	fixed-interval polling pass produces a row for each petition it weighed
28	— accepted, deferred, declined, or held without a state change — so a
29	patron, a downstream petitioner, or a future ethnographer can ask "did
30	the Reckoner ever look at this petition, and what did it decide?" with
31	a single book query.
32	
33	This doc settles the schema, the index set, the retention stance, the
34	CDC attachment, and the conceptual framing relative to the
35	petition-state book that lives next door. It is decision-supporting
36	prose only — downstream Reckoner-core implementers consume this design
37	as a settled contract; no code, schema declaration, plugin manifest, or
38	`guild.json` block ships from this commission.
39	
40	---
41	
42	## Book Identity
43	
44	`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')` — `ownerId` is `reckoner`, the
45	plugin id of the new Reckoner-core; book name is the plural noun
46	`reckonings`, matching the sibling event-log naming convention
47	(`events`, `pulses`, `sessions`, `transcripts`).
48	
49	The auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks event surface for this book is
50	therefore `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
51	`book.reckoner.reckonings.updated`, and
52	`book.reckoner.reckonings.deleted`. Of these only the `.created` event
53	ever fires in practice — see the Immutability section below — and that
54	single event is the channel petitioners subscribe to (see CDC
55	Attachment).
56	
57	---
58	
59	## Record Schema
60	
61	### Outcome enum
62	
63	Every Reckonings record carries a four-state `outcome`:
64	
65	```
66	'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op'
67	```
68	
69	- **accepted** — the Reckoner created a writ for the petition and
70	  flipped the petition state to `accepted`. The resulting writ flows
71	  into the standard rig pipeline (Distiller → Sage → Artificer), same
72	  as a patron-posted commission.
73	- **deferred** — the Reckoner held the petition until a wake-up signal
74	  fires, with structured reason metadata (see below).
75	- **declined** — the Reckoner rejected the petition on validity
76	  grounds. The decline scope is intentionally narrow (see Decline
77	  Reasons) — "this work isn't worth doing on merit" is patron
78	  territory and produces a `withdrawn` transition by the patron, not
79	  a Reckoner-issued decline.
80	- **no-op** — the Reckoner weighed the petition this tick but produced
81	  no state transition. Deferred petitions whose wake-up signal has not
82	  yet fired are the typical case; so are pending petitions blocked
83	  behind a higher-priority sibling. No-ops are first-class — see No-op
84	  Handling.
85	
86	`withdrawn` is a petitioner-initiated transition and is **not** a
87	Reckoner-consideration outcome, so it does not produce a Reckonings
88	row.
89	
90	### Petition shape (settled upstream)
91	
92	The Reckoner reads petitions from the petition-state book; the schema
93	of that book is settled by an adjacent commission and reproduced here
94	so this doc is self-contained. Each petition carries:
95	
96	- `source` — petitioner identity (vision-keeper, future tech-debt
97	  watchers, the laboratory introspection writer, ad-hoc patron
98	  petitions, …).
99	- `intent` — short imperative sentence describing the work the
100	  petitioner wants done.
101	- `rationale` — long-form prose justifying the petition.
102	- `priority_signals` — a structured block: `urgency` (an enum,
103	  `immediate | urgent | normal | low`) plus an optional
104	  `strategic_ref` pointing into a vision-document or roadmap entry.
105	- `context_anchors` — file paths, writ ids, transcript ids, and
106	  similar handles a downstream specifier can use as starting points.
107	
108	There is no pre-built brief or spec on a petition. Specification work
109	happens **after** acceptance through the existing Distiller → Sage
110	pipeline, the same path patron-posted commissions take.
111	
112	### Petition lifecycle (settled upstream)
113	
114	```
115	new → pending → { accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn }
116	```
117	
118	- `accepted`, `declined`, and `withdrawn` are terminal.
119	- `deferred → pending` re-awakens on a wake-up signal.
120	- `withdrawn` is petitioner-initiated and produces **no** Reckonings
121	  row — the Reckoner never weighed it as part of its own
122	  consideration loop.
123	
124	### Record body
125	
126	```typescript
127	// Illustrative — declared in the Reckoner-core commission, not here.
128	interface ReckoningDoc {
129	  /** Unique id (`rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`). Sortable by creation time. */
130	  id: string;
131	
132	  /** The petition this record is about. */
133	  petitionId: string;
134	
135	  /** Lean denormalized projection — see "Lean snapshot" below. */
136	  source: string;
137	  urgency: 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';
138	
139	  /** Outcome enum — drives the discriminated-union reason fields. */
140	  outcome: 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';
141	
142	  /**
143	   * Triggering Clockworks event id — the `schedule.fired` row that
144	   * produced this tick. Optional only on the patron fast path.
145	   * See "Tick identity".
146	   */
147	  tickEventId?: string;
148	
149	  /** ISO timestamp when the Reckoner completed this consideration. */
150	  consideredAt: string;
151	
152	  // ── Outcome-keyed reason metadata (flat optionals) ──────────────
153	  // Consumer types should encode the iff-outcome invariant via a
154	  // discriminated union; the persisted shape stays flat so indexes
155	  // can name top-level fields directly.
156	
157	  /** outcome === 'declined' */
158	  declineReason?:
159	    | 'malformed'
160	    | 'duplicate'
161	    | 'policy_violation'
162	    | 'source_banned'
163	    | 'other';
164	  remediationHint?: string;
165	
166	  /** outcome === 'deferred' */
167	  deferReason?:
168	    | 'priority'
169	    | 'queue_depth'
170	    | 'time_hold'
171	    | 'patron_policy'
172	    | 'other';
173	  deferUntil?: string;            // ISO timestamp, optional
174	  deferSignal?: string;           // event-pattern reservation, optional
175	  deferCount?: number;            // running count of times deferred
176	  firstDeferredAt?: string;       // ISO timestamp
177	  lastDeferredAt?: string;        // ISO timestamp
178	  deferNote?: string;             // freeform short note
179	
180	  /** outcome === 'accepted' */
181	  writId?: string;                // the writ created on acceptance
182	  acceptedAt?: string;            // ISO timestamp
183	}
184	```
185	
186	### Identity (`id`)
187	
188	Record ids use the Stacks `generateId('rk')` convention, producing the
189	form `rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`. The two-letter prefix is a deliberate
190	choice:
191	
192	- Single-letter prefixes (`p-` for pulses, `w-` for writs, `e-` for
193	  events, `d-` for dispatches, `c-` for commissions) are taken.
194	- The next plausible single letter (`r-`) is one keystroke away from
195	  Spider's `rig-` prefix and would invite confusion in CLI surfaces
196	  where ids are pasted side-by-side.
197	- `rk-` keeps the prefix short while being unambiguous against
198	  `rig-`. The same logic applies to any future `rc-` (Reckoner
199	  control plane) artifact — every Reckoner-domain id starts with `r`,
200	  the second letter discriminates.
201	
202	The base36-timestamp prefix sorts consistently with `createdAt` for
203	same-millisecond emissions, so list views ordered by `createdAt desc,
204	id desc` produce stable output across repeated calls — same precedent
205	the Lattice uses for pulse-id tie-breaking.
206	
207	### Lean snapshot vs. fat denormalization
208	
209	The record carries `petitionId` plus a small, deliberate projection of
210	two petition fields — `source` and `priority_signals.urgency` — at the
211	top level. It does **not** embed the full petition body and does
212	**not** carry a foreign-key-only reference to the petition row.
213	
214	The two projected fields are the ones that drive hot filter queries:
215	
216	- "show me everything the Reckoner did with petitions from
217	  `vision-keeper`" — `source` filter.
218	- "show me every consideration of an `urgent` petition since T" —
219	  `urgency` and `consideredAt` filter.
220	
221	Embedding only these two avoids a join on every per-petition timeline
222	or per-source audit query, while keeping the record byte-budget close
223	to the lattice/pulses precedent. The full petition row remains the
224	source of truth; the Reckonings record is a snapshot at the moment of
225	consideration.
226	
227	The alternative (a fat record carrying `intent`, `rationale`,
228	`context_anchors`, etc.) was considered and rejected: those fields are
229	not filtered against, they grow with the petition's free-form prose,
230	and they would inflate every row of an append-forever book. The
231	animator's lean-record / heavy-blob split (sessions + transcripts) is
232	the precedent — but Reckonings stays as a single book because the
233	record body is already lean enough that a heavy-blob sibling would be
234	empty.
235	
236	### Tick identity
237	
238	The Reckoner's v0 trigger is a fixed-interval polling tick implemented
239	as a Clockworks `schedule:` standing order. Every fire writes a
240	synthesized `schedule.fired` event row into `clockworks/events` (see
241	[clockworks.md → Scheduled Standing Orders](clockworks.md#scheduled-standing-orders))
242	with a unique event id of the form `e-<base36_ts>-<hex>`.
243	
244	Each Reckonings record stamps the triggering `schedule.fired` event id
245	into a `tickEventId` field. Together with `consideredAt`, this gives
246	the consumer two complementary handles:
247	
248	- **`tickEventId`** — exact-match join to the dispatch row, the
249	  schedule entry, and every sibling Reckonings row produced by the
250	  same tick.
251	- **`consideredAt`** — time-range filter for since-T sweeps and
252	  per-petition timeline ordering without going through the events
253	  book.
254	
255	The doc deliberately reuses the framework-emitted `schedule.fired` id
256	rather than synthesizing a new "Reckoner tick id" — it earns no second
257	piece of identity. There is one exception:
258	
259	#### Patron fast-path: `tickEventId` is optional
260	
261	The settled patron fast-path bypasses the Reckoner tick entirely. A
262	patron-posted petition with `urgency: 'immediate'` is auto-accepted at
263	post time, with the writ created synchronously and no waiting for the
264	next tick. The Reckonings row for an `immediate` acceptance is still
265	written so the audit trail is uniform — but because the row is written
266	**outside** any tick, there is no `schedule.fired` event id to stamp.
267	`tickEventId` is therefore optional, not required: present on every
268	non-fast-path row, absent on patron-fast-path acceptances.
269	
270	### Outcome-keyed reason metadata layout
271	
272	All reason fields live at the top level of the record (flat
273	optionals), with an iff-outcome invariant the consumer types encode as
274	a discriminated union:
275	
276	| Outcome     | Top-level fields populated                                                                                  |
277	|-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
278	| `accepted`  | `writId`, `acceptedAt`                                                                                       |
279	| `deferred`  | `deferReason`, `deferUntil?`, `deferSignal?`, `deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, `lastDeferredAt`, `deferNote?` |
280	| `declined`  | `declineReason`, `remediationHint?`                                                                          |
281	| `no-op`     | (none — only the projection and the tick stamp)                                                              |
282	
283	The flat layout is required so the index set (see Indexes) can name
284	top-level fields directly — `declineReason` for the "decline-by-reason
285	audit" query is the pivotal example. A nested `reason: { … }` blob
286	would force every query against reason metadata to filter in-process
287	on JSON-extracted values, defeating the index-driven design.
288	
289	This matches the lattice/pulses convention of putting filterable
290	fields at the top level and reserving free-form context for a
291	purpose-built blob; the Reckonings record has no free-form blob
292	because every meaningful field is named and filterable.
293	
294	### Decline reasons (settled upstream)
295	
296	The decline-reason enum is intentionally narrow:
297	
298	```
299	'malformed'        // petition shape is invalid (missing source, etc.)
300	'duplicate'        // a prior petition with the same intent is open
301	'policy_violation' // petition violates a guild-declared policy
302	'source_banned'    // the source has been blacklisted
303	'other'            // freeform — `remediationHint` carries the detail
304	```
305	
306	The narrowness is the point: the Reckoner's decline scope is "petition
307	validity," not "merit." A petition that is well-formed but unwise
308	gets a `withdrawn` transition from the patron — the Reckoner never
309	issues a merit-based decline.
310	
311	### Defer reasons (settled upstream)
312	
313	```
314	'priority'       // a higher-priority petition is ahead in the queue
315	'queue_depth'    // the active rig set is full; hold for capacity
316	'time_hold'      // operator-set hold until a wall-clock time
317	'patron_policy'  // guild-policy says this category is paused
318	'other'          // freeform — `deferNote` carries the detail
319	```
320	
321	`deferUntil` is populated when the Reckoner can name a wall-clock
322	time at which to revisit (a `time_hold` or a delayed-priority
323	re-evaluation). `deferSignal` is populated when the Reckoner reserves
324	an event pattern as a wake-up trigger (e.g. "re-weigh when
325	`book.reckoner.petitions.updated` fires for this petition"). At least
326	one of `deferUntil` and `deferSignal` is populated on a deferred row
327	in normal operation; both being empty is allowed for the rare
328	`other`-reason hold but should produce a `deferNote` for the audit
329	trail.
330	
331	`deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, and `lastDeferredAt` are running
332	counters: each new deferral on the same petition increments
333	`deferCount` and refreshes `lastDeferredAt`, while
334	`firstDeferredAt` is preserved across deferrals as the petition's
335	first-seen-as-deferred timestamp. The Reckoner reads the prior
336	Reckonings row for the petition to compute the running counter; the
337	journal is its own source of truth for the deferral history.
338	
339	### Acceptance metadata
340	
341	A row with `outcome: 'accepted'` carries:
342	
343	- `writId` — the Clerk writ id the Reckoner created on acceptance.
344	  This is a forward link into the rig pipeline; the writ flows
345	  through Distiller → Sage → Artificer the same as any patron-posted
346	  commission.
347	- `acceptedAt` — ISO timestamp of the acceptance, distinct from
348	  `consideredAt` only in the patron-fast-path case where the
349	  acceptance happens outside a tick. In the normal case the two are
350	  identical.
351	
352	---
353	
354	## No-op Handling
355	
356	**Every consideration produces a row, including no-ops.** The
357	Reckoner does not silently drop a tick where it weighed a petition
358	without producing a transition.
359	
360	The single-audit-trail-truth argument is the rationale: a petitioner
361	asking "did the Reckoner ever look at me?" should get the answer from
362	one query against the Reckonings book, not from a join across the
363	petition-state book and the events book and a heuristic. Splitting
364	the journal into "considered with state change" and "considered
365	without state change" — keeping the second set in some sibling
366	overlay or reconstructing it from the schedule.fired stream — would
367	duplicate the journaling work, complicate every consumer query, and
368	push reasoning about completeness onto callers.
369	
370	The journal is the audit trail; the petition-state book is not asked
371	to double as event journal.
372	
373	### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records
374	
375	A no-op row carries `source` and `urgency` exactly like every other
376	outcome — same lean snapshot, same tick stamp, same `consideredAt`.
377	The modest byte savings of a stripped no-op shape don't justify
378	branching the read path for every consumer query: filters like "since
379	T, all considerations of urgent petitions" must work uniformly across
380	all four outcomes, and the index that supports that filter
381	(`urgency`-keyed, paired with `consideredAt` in the compound
382	`['outcome', 'consideredAt']`) needs every row to be the same shape.
383	
384	This uniformity also keeps the discriminated-union consumer type
385	clean: no fork between "full record" and "stub record"; the
386	discriminant is `outcome`, and the optional reason fields default to
387	absent.
388	
389	---
390	
391	## Retention
392	
393	**Append-only forever.** The Reckonings book has no rolling-window
394	default, no built-in archival relay, no prune job. Every consideration
395	the Reckoner ever weighs persists until an explicit operator action
396	removes it.
397	
398	This matches the sibling event-log books — `clockworks/events`,
399	`clockworks/event_dispatches`, `lattice/pulses`,
400	`animator/sessions`, `animator/transcripts` all default to unbounded
401	growth. The lattice-apparatus and animator-apparatus docs both name
402	retention as a future commission with no current trigger; the
403	Reckonings book inherits the same stance.
404	
405	A rolling-window default would silently lose audit history. The
406	journal's load-bearing job is "show me everything that ever happened
407	to this petition" — a 90-day window quietly drops the row a
408	post-mortem six months later needs.
409	
410	### Storage-growth math
411	
412	The worst-case ceiling, based on the settled v0 cadence and a
413	representative pending-set size:
414	
415	```
416	1 tick / minute  ×  10 petitions weighed per tick  ×  365 days
417	  = 5,256,000 rows / year
418	  ≈ 2.5 GB / year   at  ~500 bytes / row (lean projection)
419	  ≈ 10.5 GB / year  at  ~2 KB  / row (with full deferral metadata)
420	```
421	
422	SQLite handles 5M-row tables cleanly with the indexes declared below
423	— primary-key lookups stay sub-millisecond, the indexed range scans
424	on `consideredAt` and the per-petition timeline use the compound
425	indexes directly, and table size at this magnitude is well within the
426	Animator's transcripts-book scale (~30–300 MB / day) that the same
427	substrate is already exercised at.
428	
429	The math shows we are not in trouble. It also identifies the
430	trip-wires that warrant revisiting: a 10× cadence, a 10× pending-set
431	size, or a sustained 6-month measurement that diverges materially
432	from this projection. The trip-wires belong in Open Questions, not
433	in the v0 retention design.
434	
435	### Future archival pattern (named, not built)
436	
437	When an operator does need to bound the book, the natural shape — to
438	be designed by a future commission, not this one — is a separate
439	archival relay subscribed to a wall-clock schedule that:
440	
441	1. Selects rows older than a configured threshold via a compound
442	   index (`['consideredAt']`).
443	2. Writes them to a sibling `reckonings_archive` book, an external
444	   sink (the Sentinel apparatus, the Laboratory's CDC ingestion), or
445	   both.
446	3. Deletes the live rows in the same transaction the archive write
447	   commits in.
448	
449	The doc names this only as an opt-in; building it before the
450	trip-wires fire would be premature.
451	
452	---
453	
454	## Query Patterns and Indexes
455	
456	The index set is filter-shape-driven: every entry traces back to a
457	named query the Reckoner or its consumers run. Speculative indexes
458	are not added.
459	
460	### Named queries
461	
462	| Query | Filter shape | Index used |
463	|-------|--------------|------------|
464	| **Per-petition timeline** — show every consideration of petition X, oldest first | `petitionId = X` ORDER BY `consideredAt asc` | `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']` |
465	| **Since-T sweep** — every Reckonings row produced since timestamp T | `consideredAt >= T` | `consideredAt` |
466	| **Decline-by-reason audit** — every petition declined for reason R | `outcome = 'declined' AND declineReason = R` | `declineReason` (with the `outcome` filter narrowing the candidate set further) |
467	| **Per-source filtering** — every consideration of petitions emitted by source S | `source = S` (optionally + `consideredAt`) | `source` |
468	| **Recent-by-outcome** — most recent N rows for outcome O | `outcome = O` ORDER BY `consideredAt desc` | `['outcome', 'consideredAt']` |
469	| **Urgency-filtered timeline** — recent considerations of urgent petitions | `outcome = O AND consideredAt >= T` (with in-process urgency narrow), or via the same compound | `['outcome', 'consideredAt']` |
470	| **Outcome-only filter** — count or list rows by outcome | `outcome = O` | `outcome` |
471	
472	### Declared index set
473	
474	```typescript
475	indexes: [
476	  'petitionId',
477	  'consideredAt',
478	  'outcome',
479	  'source',
480	  'declineReason',
481	  ['outcome', 'consideredAt'],
482	  ['petitionId', 'consideredAt'],
483	]
484	```
485	
486	Tracing each entry back to the queries it supports:
487	
488	- **`petitionId`** — bare-key existence checks ("does any row exist
489	  for this petition?") and the foreign-key style join from the
490	  petition-state book; superseded for ordered timelines by
491	  `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`.
492	- **`consideredAt`** — the unconditional since-T sweep, used by
493	  recent-history surfaces (Oculus pages, the future Sentinel
494	  apparatus's archival selector, vision-keeper's "what changed since
495	  my last poll" check).
496	- **`outcome`** — outcome-only filters and counts; standalone for
497	  uses that don't need a time bound.
498	- **`source`** — per-source audit (every consideration of petitions
499	  from a single petitioner). The petitioners-registration commission
500	  will likely want to surface this as part of an operator's
501	  per-petitioner page.
502	- **`declineReason`** — the decline-by-reason audit. The flat
503	  schema layout (D10) is what makes this an indexable top-level
504	  field; a nested `reason: { … }` would not.
505	- **`['outcome', 'consideredAt']`** — recent-by-outcome compound,
506	  serving the most common dashboard query: "what did the Reckoner
507	  decide in the last hour, grouped by outcome." The leading
508	  `outcome` column is low-cardinality (4 values), so this index
509	  doubles as a fast histogram input.
510	- **`['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`** — per-petition timeline
511	  ordering without a re-sort. Critical for the petitioner-side "show
512	  me my petition's full history" view.
513	
514	`urgency` is not indexed in v0. It is filterable in-process from any
515	of the time-or-outcome-led results, and the dashboards currently
516	described do not lead with urgency. If a future query shape leads
517	with `urgency` (e.g. an Oculus widget that sweeps "every immediate
518	petition the Reckoner has ever weighed"), adding `urgency` or the
519	compound `['urgency', 'consideredAt']` is the natural follow-up.
520	
521	`tickEventId` is similarly not indexed. The expected access pattern
522	is "look up the Reckonings row by id, then walk to the tick" — not
523	"find every row produced by tick T," which is rare and tolerates the
524	full scan or a join through `consideredAt`. Adding the index later
525	costs one schema migration and earns its keep only if the access
526	pattern changes.
527	
528	---
529	
530	## Immutability
531	
532	Reckonings records are **strictly immutable.** They carry `consideredAt`
533	and no `updatedAt`; once written, the row is never patched.
534	
535	### Why no `updatedAt`
536	
537	The Lattice's `pulses` book carries `updatedAt` because pulses have a
538	mutable field — `deliveryState` (`pending → delivered/failed`) — that
539	the Lattice itself rewrites as dispatches progress. The Reckonings
540	book has no analogous mutable field. There is no producer that would
541	mutate a record in place:
542	
543	- The outcome is final at consideration time.
544	- The reason metadata is derived from the petition state at the
545	  moment of consideration; a later re-evaluation produces a new row,
546	  not a patch on the old one.
547	- The `writId` for an accepted petition does not change.
548	- Defer-counter updates (`deferCount`, `lastDeferredAt`) accumulate
549	  by writing a new deferred row, not by mutating the prior one — the
550	  per-deferral history is the journal's load-bearing output.
551	
552	Adding `updatedAt` with no producer would be structure with no
553	consumer.
554	
555	### Future mutation needs
556	
557	If a future commission needs to attach mutable annotations to a
558	Reckonings row — operator notes, post-hoc tags, classifications from
559	a Sentinel-style auditor — those belong in a sibling overlay book
560	keyed by Reckonings id, not in-place mutation of the journal. The
561	journal is the durable record; overlays are layered on top.
562	
563	---
564	
565	## CDC Attachment
566	
567	### Channel name
568	
569	Petitioner subscribers wire to the auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks
570	event:
571	
572	```
573	book.reckoner.reckonings.created
574	```
575	
576	The change-event payload is the Stacks `ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>`
577	passed through verbatim by the Clockworks CDC bridge (see
578	[clockworks.md → Book change events](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
579	and [event-catalog.md → CDC Events](../reference/event-catalog.md#cdc-events)).
580	Subscribers that want to filter to a specific outcome do so in their
581	relay handler, against `event.entry.outcome`:
582	
583	```typescript
584	// Illustrative — vision-keeper's accepted-only relay sketch.
585	relay({
586	  name: 'vision-keeper-on-accept',
587	  handler: async (event) => {
588	    const change = event.payload as ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>;
589	    if (change.type !== 'create') return;
590	    if (change.entry.outcome !== 'accepted') return;
591	    // … react to the accepted petition.
592	  },
593	});
594	```
595	
596	The in-relay payload filter is one line. Adding a named-events surface
597	(`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, `reckoning.declined`,
598	`reckoning.no-op`) would double the write path with no second consumer
599	auto-wiring cannot serve. Named events are an explicitly considered
600	and rejected alternative; see Open Questions for the conditions under
601	which they would earn their keep.
602	
603	### No carve-out from auto-wiring
604	
605	The Clockworks apparatus carves `clockworks/events` itself out of CDC
606	auto-wiring because a watcher on the events book would observe its
607	own emit and re-emit forever — see
608	[clockworks.ts: auto-wiring carve-out](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/clockworks.ts).
609	Reckonings has **no** such recursion: the Reckoner does not write a
610	Reckonings row in response to a Reckonings change event, and no
611	downstream petitioner loops back into the Reckoner's tick path
612	through the Reckonings stream.
613	
614	The Reckonings book therefore takes **no carve-out**. The auto-wired
615	`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` event fires normally and is the
616	single CDC channel petitioners subscribe to.
617	
618	If the doc adopted named events instead, the carve-out question would
619	be moot — but then we would be writing every change twice (once via
620	auto-wire, once via the named emit). Keeping the Reckonings book in
621	the auto-wired path is what makes the single-write design work.
622	
623	### Validator namespace observation
624	
625	The `reckoner.` and `reckoning.` prefixes are **not** currently
626	reserved in the Clockworks signal validator
627	(`packages/plugins/clockworks/src/signal-validator.ts` —
628	`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` covers `anima.`, `commission.`, `tool.`,
629	`migration.`, `guild.`, `standing-order.`, `session.`, `schedule.`).
630	This is **not load-bearing in v0**: the auto-wired
631	`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` channel is the only event the
632	Reckonings book produces, and the `book.` prefix is also unreserved
633	(see [event-catalog.md: reserved-namespace gap](../reference/event-catalog.md#reserved-namespaces))
634	so adding `reckoner.` to the reserved list would not close the
635	existing gap on CDC events anyway.
636	
637	If a future commission earns the named-events surface (the
638	alternative rejected here), it must extend the validator's
639	`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` list at the same time so animas cannot
640	forge `reckoning.accepted` / `reckoning.deferred` / etc. via the
641	`signal` tool. That coupling is owned by whichever future commission
642	introduces named emission — it is **not** done by this design.
643	
644	---
645	
646	## Journal vs. Materialized View
647	
648	The Reckoner ecosystem uses two books with deliberately different
649	grains:
650	
651	| Book                          | Role                | Grain                                        |
652	|-------------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------------|
653	| `reckoner/petitions` (separate commission) | Materialized view   | One row per petition, current state, fast lookup |
654	| `reckoner/reckonings` (this doc) | Event journal       | One row per consideration, full history       |
655	
656	**The two CDC channels are not duplicative.** They differ in grain:
657	
658	- `book.reckoner.petitions.{created,updated,deleted}` fires on
659	  state-transitions of a petition. A subscriber wiring to
660	  `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` and filtering on
661	  `event.entry.state === 'accepted'` sees the **state-of-the-world**
662	  change at the moment of acceptance, but does not see the
663	  Reckoner's repeated weighings of that same petition while it was
664	  pending or deferred.
665	- `book.reckoner.reckonings.created` fires on every consideration —
666	  state-changing or not. A subscriber wiring here sees the
667	  **decision history** of every petition the Reckoner has weighed.
668	
669	Asking the petition-state book to double as a journal would force an
670	O(n) scan of every petition row to reconstruct deferral history, and
671	would fail entirely for no-op considerations that produced no
672	state-transition row to scan.
673	
674	Asking the journal to double as a materialized view would force every
675	"what is the current state of petition X?" query to walk the journal
676	backward looking for the most recent terminal row. The petition row
677	has its own state machine and its own metadata (deferral counters,
678	strategic-ref, withdraw-trigger handles); it is **not** merely
679	derived from the journal.
680	
681	The two books exist together. Petitioners that want "act on every
682	state transition" subscribe to the petitions CDC channel.
683	Petitioners that want "audit every consideration" subscribe to the
684	reckonings CDC channel. Most petitioners only need the first. The
685	journal is the second.
686	
687	---
688	
689	## Precedent Comparison
690	
691	The Reckonings book is the fifth book in the guild's family of
692	event-log substrates. It inherits the established shape from its
693	predecessors and diverges where the load-bearing constraints differ.
694	The four citations:
695	
696	- `clockworks/events` — `EventDoc` in
697	  [packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts)
698	  (`{ id, name, payload, emitter, firedAt, processed }`), index set
699	  `['name', 'processed', 'firedAt', ['processed', 'firedAt']]`.
700	- `clockworks/event_dispatches` — `EventDispatchDoc` in the same
701	  file (`{ id, eventId, handlerType, handlerName, targetRole,
702	  noticeType, startedAt, endedAt, status, error }`), index set
703	  `['eventId', 'status', ['eventId', 'status']]`.
704	- `lattice/pulses` — `PulseDoc` in
705	  [packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts)
706	  (`{ id, source, triggerType, writId, title, summary, linkUrl,
707	  context, deliveryState, deliveryError?, createdAt, updatedAt }`),
708	  index set `['triggerType', 'source', 'createdAt', 'deliveryState',
709	  'writId', ['deliveryState', 'createdAt'], ['triggerType',
710	  'createdAt']]`.
711	- `animator/sessions` and `animator/transcripts` — `SessionDoc` and
712	  `TranscriptDoc` in `packages/plugins/animator/src/types.ts`, index
713	  sets `['startedAt', 'status', 'conversationId', 'provider']` and
714	  `['sessionId']`.
715	
716	### What Reckonings inherits
717	
718	| From | Lesson |
719	|------|--------|
720	| `clockworks/events` | The `id`-format convention (`<prefix>-<base36_ts>-<hex>` via `generateId`); the append-only stance; the framework-emitter trust convention (the writer stamps `source` / `emitter`, no validation). |
721	| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | The filter-shape-driven index set — only fields that name a real query get an index; compound indexes are added when the query is `WHERE a = … ORDER BY b` shaped. |
722	| `lattice/pulses` | The top-level-fields-plus-typed-context shape (the projection lives at the top so it is filterable, the free-form payload lives in a nested blob); Phase 2 CDC for downstream dispatch (the `book.<owner>.<book>.created` channel petitioners subscribe to is the same shape Lattice consumers use); the lean record body. |
723	| `animator/sessions` | The lean-record / heavy-blob distinction at the apparatus level — even though Reckonings keeps a single book, the framing of "every field is filterable; nothing here is a free-form blob" is what makes the single-book choice work. |
724	
725	### Where Reckonings diverges
726	
727	| From | Divergence | Why |
728	|------|------------|-----|
729	| `clockworks/events` | **No carve-out from CDC auto-wiring.** Events carves itself out because a watcher on the events book observes its own emit. Reckonings has no such recursion. | See CDC Attachment. |
730	| `lattice/pulses` | **No `updatedAt`.** Pulses mutate `deliveryState`; Reckonings has no mutable field. | See Immutability. |
731	| `lattice/pulses` | **No free-form `context: Record<string, unknown>`.** Every Reckonings field is named and indexed-or-filtered-against. | The pulses-style context blob exists because pulse consumers want trigger-keyed structured payloads (e.g. `WritStuckContext`); Reckonings reason metadata is already discriminated by `outcome`, so a separate context blob would be empty. |
732	| `animator/sessions` + `animator/transcripts` | **One book, not two.** Sessions is lean, transcripts is heavy; Reckonings keeps the lean half and has no heavy-blob sibling. | The petition's `intent`, `rationale`, and `context_anchors` live on the petition row, not on the journal. The journal stays at the lean-record scale. |
733	| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | **No per-handler dispatch log.** event_dispatches is a sibling to events recording one row per handler invocation; Reckonings has no per-handler concept. | Petitioner subscribers run as ordinary Clockworks standing orders against the auto-wired CDC event, so the dispatch log they produce already lives in `clockworks/event_dispatches`. A second per-handler log on the Reckonings side would duplicate that. |
734	
735	The dual-table pattern (events + event_dispatches) was considered as a
736	shape for the Reckoner — one table for petitions weighed, a second
737	for "what each petitioner did about it." It was rejected on the same
738	grounds: petitioner reactions are Clockworks standing orders, and the
739	existing `event_dispatches` book already records one row per
740	invocation. Adding a Reckoner-owned dispatch log would re-implement a
741	sibling apparatus's load-bearing data.
742	
743	---
744	
745	## Open Questions
746	
747	The four items this design intentionally leaves for follow-on
748	resolution.
749	
750	- **Petitioner registration.** A parallel commission
751	  (`docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md`) is in flight and
752	  will define how a petitioner declares itself to the Reckoner — the
753	  registration contract, the per-source policy block, the source-id
754	  validator. This Reckonings doc names the auto-wired event surface
755	  registration will plug into (`book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
756	  with the registered petitioner's standing order filtering on
757	  `outcome` and `source`), but does **not** specify the registration
758	  shape. When the registration commission lands, the per-source
759	  filtering convention should be cross-referenced from here.
760	
761	- **Retention trip-wires.** v0 ships append-only forever with the
762	  storage-growth math above. The trip-wires that warrant a future
763	  retention-design commission are concrete:
764	  1. A Sentinel or Laboratory archival sink lands and an operator
765	     wants to point an archival relay at it.
766	  2. An operator complains about Reckonings book size or about
767	     Stacks query latency on the per-petition timeline query.
768	  3. Six months of production-growth measurement validates the
769	     5.26M rows / year projection (or, more interestingly, refutes
770	     it by 2× or more in either direction).
771	  Until one of those fires, retention is "do nothing." Designing the
772	  archive shape before it has a pull is premature; the future
773	  archival pattern in the Retention section is the placeholder, not
774	  the design.
775	
776	- **Queue-observer fold-back.** The existing
777	  `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` is a narrow CDC observer that
778	  emits `reckoner.writ-stuck` / `reckoner.writ-failed` /
779	  `reckoner.queue-drained` Lattice pulses (see
780	  [apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md)). It was renamed
781	  out of the `reckoner` plugin id so that id can be reused for the
782	  new petition-scheduling Reckoner. An open question is whether the
783	  queue-observer's responsibilities (writ-lifecycle observation
784	  feeding Lattice pulses) fold back into the new Reckoner core, or
785	  stay in the renamed sibling. The Reckonings book is unaffected either way —
786	  the queue-observer's pulses go to the Lattice, not to the
787	  Reckonings book — but the conceptual seam between "petition
788	  evaluation" and "writ-lifecycle observation" wants to be settled
789	  before the new Reckoner core ships.
790	
791	- **When named events earn their keep.** D8 rejects a named-events
792	  emission surface (`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, etc.)
793	  on the grounds that auto-wiring + a one-line in-relay outcome
794	  filter serves every consumer auto-wiring can serve. The conditions
795	  under which named events earn their keep:
796	  1. A non-Clockworks consumer of the Reckonings stream appears —
797	     e.g. an external webhook bus, a Discord channel, an HTTP
798	     subscriber — that cannot conveniently consume the framework's
799	     `ChangeEvent<T>` shape and would benefit from a named event
800	     with a flat payload.
801	  2. A measurable performance penalty appears in the in-relay filter
802	     path. Today the filter is one branch on a tagged union; if a
803	     future profiling pass shows the change-event payload is large
804	     enough that all-relays-receive-all-events-and-filter is
805	     measurably worse than per-outcome dispatch, the surface
806	     justifies its own emit path.
807	  3. The validator's reserved-namespace policy gains teeth on
808	     `book.*` events and the same pass extends teeth to `reckoning.*`.
809	  Until at least one of those fires, the auto-wired single channel
810	  remains sufficient.
811	
812	---
813	
814	## See Also
815	
816	- [docs/architecture/clockworks.md](clockworks.md) — the CDC
817	  auto-wiring contract and the reserved-namespace policy.
818	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md) —
819	  the closest schema precedent for a Reckonings record.
820	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md)
821	  — the lean-record / heavy-blob book split (the alternative
822	  considered and rejected here).
823	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md)
824	  — the existing queue-observer the new Reckoner shares an id
825	  with after the rename.
826	- [docs/reference/event-catalog.md](../reference/event-catalog.md)
827	  — CDC event names, reserved namespaces, validator policy.
828	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/_template.md](apparatus/_template.md)
829	  — apparatus-doc template; this book-focused doc draws its
830	  register from it.
831

file.content:
# The Reckonings Book — Schema & CDC Contract

Status: **Draft**

Owner plugin: `reckoner` · Book name: `reckonings` · Sibling docs:
[clockworks.md](clockworks.md), [apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md),
[apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md),
[apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md).

> **⚠️ Forward-looking design.** This document specifies the
> Reckoner's evaluation-log book before the Reckoner core itself is
> commissioned. The plugin id `reckoner` and the existing
> `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` (formerly `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus`;
> a narrow queue-observer that emits Lattice pulses for `writ-stuck` /
> `writ-failed` / `queue-drained`) have been renamed in a separate
> commission so this id can be reused for the new petition-scheduling
> Reckoner. Nothing in `packages/plugins/sentinel/` is touched by this
> design — see Open Questions for the integration.

---

## Intent

The Reckonings book is the Reckoner's **evaluation journal**: one
append-only row per petition consideration, persisted to The Stacks as
`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')`. Every tick of the Reckoner's
fixed-interval polling pass produces a row for each petition it weighed
— accepted, deferred, declined, or held without a state change — so a
patron, a downstream petitioner, or a future ethnographer can ask "did
the Reckoner ever look at this petition, and what did it decide?" with
a single book query.

This doc settles the schema, the index set, the retention stance, the
CDC attachment, and the conceptual framing relative to the
petition-state book that lives next door. It is decision-supporting
prose only — downstream Reckoner-core implementers consume this design
as a settled contract; no code, schema declaration, plugin manifest, or
`guild.json` block ships from this commission.

---

## Book Identity

`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')` — `ownerId` is `reckoner`, the
plugin id of the new Reckoner-core; book name is the plural noun
`reckonings`, matching the sibling event-log naming convention
(`events`, `pulses`, `sessions`, `transcripts`).

The auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks event surface for this book is
therefore `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
`book.reckoner.reckonings.updated`, and
`book.reckoner.reckonings.deleted`. Of these only the `.created` event
ever fires in practice — see the Immutability section below — and that
single event is the channel petitioners subscribe to (see CDC
Attachment).

---

## Record Schema

### Outcome enum

Every Reckonings record carries a four-state `outcome`:

```
'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op'
```

- **accepted** — the Reckoner created a writ for the petition and
  flipped the petition state to `accepted`. The resulting writ flows
  into the standard rig pipeline (Distiller → Sage → Artificer), same
  as a patron-posted commission.
- **deferred** — the Reckoner held the petition until a wake-up signal
  fires, with structured reason metadata (see below).
- **declined** — the Reckoner rejected the petition on validity
  grounds. The decline scope is intentionally narrow (see Decline
  Reasons) — "this work isn't worth doing on merit" is patron
  territory and produces a `withdrawn` transition by the patron, not
  a Reckoner-issued decline.
- **no-op** — the Reckoner weighed the petition this tick but produced
  no state transition. Deferred petitions whose wake-up signal has not
  yet fired are the typical case; so are pending petitions blocked
  behind a higher-priority sibling. No-ops are first-class — see No-op
  Handling.

`withdrawn` is a petitioner-initiated transition and is **not** a
Reckoner-consideration outcome, so it does not produce a Reckonings
row.

### Petition shape (settled upstream)

The Reckoner reads petitions from the petition-state book; the schema
of that book is settled by an adjacent commission and reproduced here
so this doc is self-contained. Each petition carries:

- `source` — petitioner identity (vision-keeper, future tech-debt
  watchers, the laboratory introspection writer, ad-hoc patron
  petitions, …).
- `intent` — short imperative sentence describing the work the
  petitioner wants done.
- `rationale` — long-form prose justifying the petition.
- `priority_signals` — a structured block: `urgency` (an enum,
  `immediate | urgent | normal | low`) plus an optional
  `strategic_ref` pointing into a vision-document or roadmap entry.
- `context_anchors` — file paths, writ ids, transcript ids, and
  similar handles a downstream specifier can use as starting points.

There is no pre-built brief or spec on a petition. Specification work
happens **after** acceptance through the existing Distiller → Sage
pipeline, the same path patron-posted commissions take.

### Petition lifecycle (settled upstream)

```
new → pending → { accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn }
```

- `accepted`, `declined`, and `withdrawn` are terminal.
- `deferred → pending` re-awakens on a wake-up signal.
- `withdrawn` is petitioner-initiated and produces **no** Reckonings
  row — the Reckoner never weighed it as part of its own
  consideration loop.

### Record body

```typescript
// Illustrative — declared in the Reckoner-core commission, not here.
interface ReckoningDoc {
  /** Unique id (`rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`). Sortable by creation time. */
  id: string;

  /** The petition this record is about. */
  petitionId: string;

  /** Lean denormalized projection — see "Lean snapshot" below. */
  source: string;
  urgency: 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';

  /** Outcome enum — drives the discriminated-union reason fields. */
  outcome: 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';

  /**
   * Triggering Clockworks event id — the `schedule.fired` row that
   * produced this tick. Optional only on the patron fast path.
   * See "Tick identity".
   */
  tickEventId?: string;

  /** ISO timestamp when the Reckoner completed this consideration. */
  consideredAt: string;

  // ── Outcome-keyed reason metadata (flat optionals) ──────────────
  // Consumer types should encode the iff-outcome invariant via a
  // discriminated union; the persisted shape stays flat so indexes
  // can name top-level fields directly.

  /** outcome === 'declined' */
  declineReason?:
    | 'malformed'
    | 'duplicate'
    | 'policy_violation'
    | 'source_banned'
    | 'other';
  remediationHint?: string;

  /** outcome === 'deferred' */
  deferReason?:
    | 'priority'
    | 'queue_depth'
    | 'time_hold'
    | 'patron_policy'
    | 'other';
  deferUntil?: string;            // ISO timestamp, optional
  deferSignal?: string;           // event-pattern reservation, optional
  deferCount?: number;            // running count of times deferred
  firstDeferredAt?: string;       // ISO timestamp
  lastDeferredAt?: string;        // ISO timestamp
  deferNote?: string;             // freeform short note

  /** outcome === 'accepted' */
  writId?: string;                // the writ created on acceptance
  acceptedAt?: string;            // ISO timestamp
}
```

### Identity (`id`)

Record ids use the Stacks `generateId('rk')` convention, producing the
form `rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`. The two-letter prefix is a deliberate
choice:

- Single-letter prefixes (`p-` for pulses, `w-` for writs, `e-` for
  events, `d-` for dispatches, `c-` for commissions) are taken.
- The next plausible single letter (`r-`) is one keystroke away from
  Spider's `rig-` prefix and would invite confusion in CLI surfaces
  where ids are pasted side-by-side.
- `rk-` keeps the prefix short while being unambiguous against
  `rig-`. The same logic applies to any future `rc-` (Reckoner
  control plane) artifact — every Reckoner-domain id starts with `r`,
  the second letter discriminates.

The base36-timestamp prefix sorts consistently with `createdAt` for
same-millisecond emissions, so list views ordered by `createdAt desc,
id desc` produce stable output across repeated calls — same precedent
the Lattice uses for pulse-id tie-breaking.

### Lean snapshot vs. fat denormalization

The record carries `petitionId` plus a small, deliberate projection of
two petition fields — `source` and `priority_signals.urgency` — at the
top level. It does **not** embed the full petition body and does
**not** carry a foreign-key-only reference to the petition row.

The two projected fields are the ones that drive hot filter queries:

- "show me everything the Reckoner did with petitions from
  `vision-keeper`" — `source` filter.
- "show me every consideration of an `urgent` petition since T" —
  `urgency` and `consideredAt` filter.

Embedding only these two avoids a join on every per-petition timeline
or per-source audit query, while keeping the record byte-budget close
to the lattice/pulses precedent. The full petition row remains the
source of truth; the Reckonings record is a snapshot at the moment of
consideration.

The alternative (a fat record carrying `intent`, `rationale`,
`context_anchors`, etc.) was considered and rejected: those fields are
not filtered against, they grow with the petition's free-form prose,
and they would inflate every row of an append-forever book. The
animator's lean-record / heavy-blob split (sessions + transcripts) is
the precedent — but Reckonings stays as a single book because the
record body is already lean enough that a heavy-blob sibling would be
empty.

### Tick identity

The Reckoner's v0 trigger is a fixed-interval polling tick implemented
as a Clockworks `schedule:` standing order. Every fire writes a
synthesized `schedule.fired` event row into `clockworks/events` (see
[clockworks.md → Scheduled Standing Orders](clockworks.md#scheduled-standing-orders))
with a unique event id of the form `e-<base36_ts>-<hex>`.

Each Reckonings record stamps the triggering `schedule.fired` event id
into a `tickEventId` field. Together with `consideredAt`, this gives
the consumer two complementary handles:

- **`tickEventId`** — exact-match join to the dispatch row, the
  schedule entry, and every sibling Reckonings row produced by the
  same tick.
- **`consideredAt`** — time-range filter for since-T sweeps and
  per-petition timeline ordering without going through the events
  book.

The doc deliberately reuses the framework-emitted `schedule.fired` id
rather than synthesizing a new "Reckoner tick id" — it earns no second
piece of identity. There is one exception:

#### Patron fast-path: `tickEventId` is optional

The settled patron fast-path bypasses the Reckoner tick entirely. A
patron-posted petition with `urgency: 'immediate'` is auto-accepted at
post time, with the writ created synchronously and no waiting for the
next tick. The Reckonings row for an `immediate` acceptance is still
written so the audit trail is uniform — but because the row is written
**outside** any tick, there is no `schedule.fired` event id to stamp.
`tickEventId` is therefore optional, not required: present on every
non-fast-path row, absent on patron-fast-path acceptances.

### Outcome-keyed reason metadata layout

All reason fields live at the top level of the record (flat
optionals), with an iff-outcome invariant the consumer types encode as
a discriminated union:

| Outcome     | Top-level fields populated                                                                                  |
|-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `accepted`  | `writId`, `acceptedAt`                                                                                       |
| `deferred`  | `deferReason`, `deferUntil?`, `deferSignal?`, `deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, `lastDeferredAt`, `deferNote?` |
| `declined`  | `declineReason`, `remediationHint?`                                                                          |
| `no-op`     | (none — only the projection and the tick stamp)                                                              |

The flat layout is required so the index set (see Indexes) can name
top-level fields directly — `declineReason` for the "decline-by-reason
audit" query is the pivotal example. A nested `reason: { … }` blob
would force every query against reason metadata to filter in-process
on JSON-extracted values, defeating the index-driven design.

This matches the lattice/pulses convention of putting filterable
fields at the top level and reserving free-form context for a
purpose-built blob; the Reckonings record has no free-form blob
because every meaningful field is named and filterable.

### Decline reasons (settled upstream)

The decline-reason enum is intentionally narrow:

```
'malformed'        // petition shape is invalid (missing source, etc.)
'duplicate'        // a prior petition with the same intent is open
'policy_violation' // petition violates a guild-declared policy
'source_banned'    // the source has been blacklisted
'other'            // freeform — `remediationHint` carries the detail
```

The narrowness is the point: the Reckoner's decline scope is "petition
validity," not "merit." A petition that is well-formed but unwise
gets a `withdrawn` transition from the patron — the Reckoner never
issues a merit-based decline.

### Defer reasons (settled upstream)

```
'priority'       // a higher-priority petition is ahead in the queue
'queue_depth'    // the active rig set is full; hold for capacity
'time_hold'      // operator-set hold until a wall-clock time
'patron_policy'  // guild-policy says this category is paused
'other'          // freeform — `deferNote` carries the detail
```

`deferUntil` is populated when the Reckoner can name a wall-clock
time at which to revisit (a `time_hold` or a delayed-priority
re-evaluation). `deferSignal` is populated when the Reckoner reserves
an event pattern as a wake-up trigger (e.g. "re-weigh when
`book.reckoner.petitions.updated` fires for this petition"). At least
one of `deferUntil` and `deferSignal` is populated on a deferred row
in normal operation; both being empty is allowed for the rare
`other`-reason hold but should produce a `deferNote` for the audit
trail.

`deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, and `lastDeferredAt` are running
counters: each new deferral on the same petition increments
`deferCount` and refreshes `lastDeferredAt`, while
`firstDeferredAt` is preserved across deferrals as the petition's
first-seen-as-deferred timestamp. The Reckoner reads the prior
Reckonings row for the petition to compute the running counter; the
journal is its own source of truth for the deferral history.

### Acceptance metadata

A row with `outcome: 'accepted'` carries:

- `writId` — the Clerk writ id the Reckoner created on acceptance.
  This is a forward link into the rig pipeline; the writ flows
  through Distiller → Sage → Artificer the same as any patron-posted
  commission.
- `acceptedAt` — ISO timestamp of the acceptance, distinct from
  `consideredAt` only in the patron-fast-path case where the
  acceptance happens outside a tick. In the normal case the two are
  identical.

---

## No-op Handling

**Every consideration produces a row, including no-ops.** The
Reckoner does not silently drop a tick where it weighed a petition
without producing a transition.

The single-audit-trail-truth argument is the rationale: a petitioner
asking "did the Reckoner ever look at me?" should get the answer from
one query against the Reckonings book, not from a join across the
petition-state book and the events book and a heuristic. Splitting
the journal into "considered with state change" and "considered
without state change" — keeping the second set in some sibling
overlay or reconstructing it from the schedule.fired stream — would
duplicate the journaling work, complicate every consumer query, and
push reasoning about completeness onto callers.

The journal is the audit trail; the petition-state book is not asked
to double as event journal.

### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records

A no-op row carries `source` and `urgency` exactly like every other
outcome — same lean snapshot, same tick stamp, same `consideredAt`.
The modest byte savings of a stripped no-op shape don't justify
branching the read path for every consumer query: filters like "since
T, all considerations of urgent petitions" must work uniformly across
all four outcomes, and the index that supports that filter
(`urgency`-keyed, paired with `consideredAt` in the compound
`['outcome', 'consideredAt']`) needs every row to be the same shape.

This uniformity also keeps the discriminated-union consumer type
clean: no fork between "full record" and "stub record"; the
discriminant is `outcome`, and the optional reason fields default to
absent.

---

## Retention

**Append-only forever.** The Reckonings book has no rolling-window
default, no built-in archival relay, no prune job. Every consideration
the Reckoner ever weighs persists until an explicit operator action
removes it.

This matches the sibling event-log books — `clockworks/events`,
`clockworks/event_dispatches`, `lattice/pulses`,
`animator/sessions`, `animator/transcripts` all default to unbounded
growth. The lattice-apparatus and animator-apparatus docs both name
retention as a future commission with no current trigger; the
Reckonings book inherits the same stance.

A rolling-window default would silently lose audit history. The
journal's load-bearing job is "show me everything that ever happened
to this petition" — a 90-day window quietly drops the row a
post-mortem six months later needs.

### Storage-growth math

The worst-case ceiling, based on the settled v0 cadence and a
representative pending-set size:

```
1 tick / minute  ×  10 petitions weighed per tick  ×  365 days
  = 5,256,000 rows / year
  ≈ 2.5 GB / year   at  ~500 bytes / row (lean projection)
  ≈ 10.5 GB / year  at  ~2 KB  / row (with full deferral metadata)
```

SQLite handles 5M-row tables cleanly with the indexes declared below
— primary-key lookups stay sub-millisecond, the indexed range scans
on `consideredAt` and the per-petition timeline use the compound
indexes directly, and table size at this magnitude is well within the
Animator's transcripts-book scale (~30–300 MB / day) that the same
substrate is already exercised at.

The math shows we are not in trouble. It also identifies the
trip-wires that warrant revisiting: a 10× cadence, a 10× pending-set
size, or a sustained 6-month measurement that diverges materially
from this projection. The trip-wires belong in Open Questions, not
in the v0 retention design.

### Future archival pattern (named, not built)

When an operator does need to bound the book, the natural shape — to
be designed by a future commission, not this one — is a separate
archival relay subscribed to a wall-clock schedule that:

1. Selects rows older than a configured threshold via a compound
   index (`['consideredAt']`).
2. Writes them to a sibling `reckonings_archive` book, an external
   sink (the Sentinel apparatus, the Laboratory's CDC ingestion), or
   both.
3. Deletes the live rows in the same transaction the archive write
   commits in.

The doc names this only as an opt-in; building it before the
trip-wires fire would be premature.

---

## Query Patterns and Indexes

The index set is filter-shape-driven: every entry traces back to a
named query the Reckoner or its consumers run. Speculative indexes
are not added.

### Named queries

| Query | Filter shape | Index used |
|-------|--------------|------------|
| **Per-petition timeline** — show every consideration of petition X, oldest first | `petitionId = X` ORDER BY `consideredAt asc` | `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']` |
| **Since-T sweep** — every Reckonings row produced since timestamp T | `consideredAt >= T` | `consideredAt` |
| **Decline-by-reason audit** — every petition declined for reason R | `outcome = 'declined' AND declineReason = R` | `declineReason` (with the `outcome` filter narrowing the candidate set further) |
| **Per-source filtering** — every consideration of petitions emitted by source S | `source = S` (optionally + `consideredAt`) | `source` |
| **Recent-by-outcome** — most recent N rows for outcome O | `outcome = O` ORDER BY `consideredAt desc` | `['outcome', 'consideredAt']` |
| **Urgency-filtered timeline** — recent considerations of urgent petitions | `outcome = O AND consideredAt >= T` (with in-process urgency narrow), or via the same compound | `['outcome', 'consideredAt']` |
| **Outcome-only filter** — count or list rows by outcome | `outcome = O` | `outcome` |

### Declared index set

```typescript
indexes: [
  'petitionId',
  'consideredAt',
  'outcome',
  'source',
  'declineReason',
  ['outcome', 'consideredAt'],
  ['petitionId', 'consideredAt'],
]
```

Tracing each entry back to the queries it supports:

- **`petitionId`** — bare-key existence checks ("does any row exist
  for this petition?") and the foreign-key style join from the
  petition-state book; superseded for ordered timelines by
  `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`.
- **`consideredAt`** — the unconditional since-T sweep, used by
  recent-history surfaces (Oculus pages, the future Sentinel
  apparatus's archival selector, vision-keeper's "what changed since
  my last poll" check).
- **`outcome`** — outcome-only filters and counts; standalone for
  uses that don't need a time bound.
- **`source`** — per-source audit (every consideration of petitions
  from a single petitioner). The petitioners-registration commission
  will likely want to surface this as part of an operator's
  per-petitioner page.
- **`declineReason`** — the decline-by-reason audit. The flat
  schema layout (D10) is what makes this an indexable top-level
  field; a nested `reason: { … }` would not.
- **`['outcome', 'consideredAt']`** — recent-by-outcome compound,
  serving the most common dashboard query: "what did the Reckoner
  decide in the last hour, grouped by outcome." The leading
  `outcome` column is low-cardinality (4 values), so this index
  doubles as a fast histogram input.
- **`['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`** — per-petition timeline
  ordering without a re-sort. Critical for the petitioner-side "show
  me my petition's full history" view.

`urgency` is not indexed in v0. It is filterable in-process from any
of the time-or-outcome-led results, and the dashboards currently
described do not lead with urgency. If a future query shape leads
with `urgency` (e.g. an Oculus widget that sweeps "every immediate
petition the Reckoner has ever weighed"), adding `urgency` or the
compound `['urgency', 'consideredAt']` is the natural follow-up.

`tickEventId` is similarly not indexed. The expected access pattern
is "look up the Reckonings row by id, then walk to the tick" — not
"find every row produced by tick T," which is rare and tolerates the
full scan or a join through `consideredAt`. Adding the index later
costs one schema migration and earns its keep only if the access
pattern changes.

---

## Immutability

Reckonings records are **strictly immutable.** They carry `consideredAt`
and no `updatedAt`; once written, the row is never patched.

### Why no `updatedAt`

The Lattice's `pulses` book carries `updatedAt` because pulses have a
mutable field — `deliveryState` (`pending → delivered/failed`) — that
the Lattice itself rewrites as dispatches progress. The Reckonings
book has no analogous mutable field. There is no producer that would
mutate a record in place:

- The outcome is final at consideration time.
- The reason metadata is derived from the petition state at the
  moment of consideration; a later re-evaluation produces a new row,
  not a patch on the old one.
- The `writId` for an accepted petition does not change.
- Defer-counter updates (`deferCount`, `lastDeferredAt`) accumulate
  by writing a new deferred row, not by mutating the prior one — the
  per-deferral history is the journal's load-bearing output.

Adding `updatedAt` with no producer would be structure with no
consumer.

### Future mutation needs

If a future commission needs to attach mutable annotations to a
Reckonings row — operator notes, post-hoc tags, classifications from
a Sentinel-style auditor — those belong in a sibling overlay book
keyed by Reckonings id, not in-place mutation of the journal. The
journal is the durable record; overlays are layered on top.

---

## CDC Attachment

### Channel name

Petitioner subscribers wire to the auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks
event:

```
book.reckoner.reckonings.created
```

The change-event payload is the Stacks `ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>`
passed through verbatim by the Clockworks CDC bridge (see
[clockworks.md → Book change events](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
and [event-catalog.md → CDC Events](../reference/event-catalog.md#cdc-events)).
Subscribers that want to filter to a specific outcome do so in their
relay handler, against `event.entry.outcome`:

```typescript
// Illustrative — vision-keeper's accepted-only relay sketch.
relay({
  name: 'vision-keeper-on-accept',
  handler: async (event) => {
    const change = event.payload as ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>;
    if (change.type !== 'create') return;
    if (change.entry.outcome !== 'accepted') return;
    // … react to the accepted petition.
  },
});
```

The in-relay payload filter is one line. Adding a named-events surface
(`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, `reckoning.declined`,
`reckoning.no-op`) would double the write path with no second consumer
auto-wiring cannot serve. Named events are an explicitly considered
and rejected alternative; see Open Questions for the conditions under
which they would earn their keep.

### No carve-out from auto-wiring

The Clockworks apparatus carves `clockworks/events` itself out of CDC
auto-wiring because a watcher on the events book would observe its
own emit and re-emit forever — see
[clockworks.ts: auto-wiring carve-out](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/clockworks.ts).
Reckonings has **no** such recursion: the Reckoner does not write a
Reckonings row in response to a Reckonings change event, and no
downstream petitioner loops back into the Reckoner's tick path
through the Reckonings stream.

The Reckonings book therefore takes **no carve-out**. The auto-wired
`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` event fires normally and is the
single CDC channel petitioners subscribe to.

If the doc adopted named events instead, the carve-out question would
be moot — but then we would be writing every change twice (once via
auto-wire, once via the named emit). Keeping the Reckonings book in
the auto-wired path is what makes the single-write design work.

### Validator namespace observation

The `reckoner.` and `reckoning.` prefixes are **not** currently
reserved in the Clockworks signal validator
(`packages/plugins/clockworks/src/signal-validator.ts` —
`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` covers `anima.`, `commission.`, `tool.`,
`migration.`, `guild.`, `standing-order.`, `session.`, `schedule.`).
This is **not load-bearing in v0**: the auto-wired
`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` channel is the only event the
Reckonings book produces, and the `book.` prefix is also unreserved
(see [event-catalog.md: reserved-namespace gap](../reference/event-catalog.md#reserved-namespaces))
so adding `reckoner.` to the reserved list would not close the
existing gap on CDC events anyway.

If a future commission earns the named-events surface (the
alternative rejected here), it must extend the validator's
`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` list at the same time so animas cannot
forge `reckoning.accepted` / `reckoning.deferred` / etc. via the
`signal` tool. That coupling is owned by whichever future commission
introduces named emission — it is **not** done by this design.

---

## Journal vs. Materialized View

The Reckoner ecosystem uses two books with deliberately different
grains:

| Book                          | Role                | Grain                                        |
|-------------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `reckoner/petitions` (separate commission) | Materialized view   | One row per petition, current state, fast lookup |
| `reckoner/reckonings` (this doc) | Event journal       | One row per consideration, full history       |

**The two CDC channels are not duplicative.** They differ in grain:

- `book.reckoner.petitions.{created,updated,deleted}` fires on
  state-transitions of a petition. A subscriber wiring to
  `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` and filtering on
  `event.entry.state === 'accepted'` sees the **state-of-the-world**
  change at the moment of acceptance, but does not see the
  Reckoner's repeated weighings of that same petition while it was
  pending or deferred.
- `book.reckoner.reckonings.created` fires on every consideration —
  state-changing or not. A subscriber wiring here sees the
  **decision history** of every petition the Reckoner has weighed.

Asking the petition-state book to double as a journal would force an
O(n) scan of every petition row to reconstruct deferral history, and
would fail entirely for no-op considerations that produced no
state-transition row to scan.

Asking the journal to double as a materialized view would force every
"what is the current state of petition X?" query to walk the journal
backward looking for the most recent terminal row. The petition row
has its own state machine and its own metadata (deferral counters,
strategic-ref, withdraw-trigger handles); it is **not** merely
derived from the journal.

The two books exist together. Petitioners that want "act on every
state transition" subscribe to the petitions CDC channel.
Petitioners that want "audit every consideration" subscribe to the
reckonings CDC channel. Most petitioners only need the first. The
journal is the second.

---

## Precedent Comparison

The Reckonings book is the fifth book in the guild's family of
event-log substrates. It inherits the established shape from its
predecessors and diverges where the load-bearing constraints differ.
The four citations:

- `clockworks/events` — `EventDoc` in
  [packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts)
  (`{ id, name, payload, emitter, firedAt, processed }`), index set
  `['name', 'processed', 'firedAt', ['processed', 'firedAt']]`.
- `clockworks/event_dispatches` — `EventDispatchDoc` in the same
  file (`{ id, eventId, handlerType, handlerName, targetRole,
  noticeType, startedAt, endedAt, status, error }`), index set
  `['eventId', 'status', ['eventId', 'status']]`.
- `lattice/pulses` — `PulseDoc` in
  [packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts)
  (`{ id, source, triggerType, writId, title, summary, linkUrl,
  context, deliveryState, deliveryError?, createdAt, updatedAt }`),
  index set `['triggerType', 'source', 'createdAt', 'deliveryState',
  'writId', ['deliveryState', 'createdAt'], ['triggerType',
  'createdAt']]`.
- `animator/sessions` and `animator/transcripts` — `SessionDoc` and
  `TranscriptDoc` in `packages/plugins/animator/src/types.ts`, index
  sets `['startedAt', 'status', 'conversationId', 'provider']` and
  `['sessionId']`.

### What Reckonings inherits

| From | Lesson |
|------|--------|
| `clockworks/events` | The `id`-format convention (`<prefix>-<base36_ts>-<hex>` via `generateId`); the append-only stance; the framework-emitter trust convention (the writer stamps `source` / `emitter`, no validation). |
| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | The filter-shape-driven index set — only fields that name a real query get an index; compound indexes are added when the query is `WHERE a = … ORDER BY b` shaped. |
| `lattice/pulses` | The top-level-fields-plus-typed-context shape (the projection lives at the top so it is filterable, the free-form payload lives in a nested blob); Phase 2 CDC for downstream dispatch (the `book.<owner>.<book>.created` channel petitioners subscribe to is the same shape Lattice consumers use); the lean record body. |
| `animator/sessions` | The lean-record / heavy-blob distinction at the apparatus level — even though Reckonings keeps a single book, the framing of "every field is filterable; nothing here is a free-form blob" is what makes the single-book choice work. |

### Where Reckonings diverges

| From | Divergence | Why |
|------|------------|-----|
| `clockworks/events` | **No carve-out from CDC auto-wiring.** Events carves itself out because a watcher on the events book observes its own emit. Reckonings has no such recursion. | See CDC Attachment. |
| `lattice/pulses` | **No `updatedAt`.** Pulses mutate `deliveryState`; Reckonings has no mutable field. | See Immutability. |
| `lattice/pulses` | **No free-form `context: Record<string, unknown>`.** Every Reckonings field is named and indexed-or-filtered-against. | The pulses-style context blob exists because pulse consumers want trigger-keyed structured payloads (e.g. `WritStuckContext`); Reckonings reason metadata is already discriminated by `outcome`, so a separate context blob would be empty. |
| `animator/sessions` + `animator/transcripts` | **One book, not two.** Sessions is lean, transcripts is heavy; Reckonings keeps the lean half and has no heavy-blob sibling. | The petition's `intent`, `rationale`, and `context_anchors` live on the petition row, not on the journal. The journal stays at the lean-record scale. |
| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | **No per-handler dispatch log.** event_dispatches is a sibling to events recording one row per handler invocation; Reckonings has no per-handler concept. | Petitioner subscribers run as ordinary Clockworks standing orders against the auto-wired CDC event, so the dispatch log they produce already lives in `clockworks/event_dispatches`. A second per-handler log on the Reckonings side would duplicate that. |

The dual-table pattern (events + event_dispatches) was considered as a
shape for the Reckoner — one table for petitions weighed, a second
for "what each petitioner did about it." It was rejected on the same
grounds: petitioner reactions are Clockworks standing orders, and the
existing `event_dispatches` book already records one row per
invocation. Adding a Reckoner-owned dispatch log would re-implement a
sibling apparatus's load-bearing data.

---

## Open Questions

The four items this design intentionally leaves for follow-on
resolution.

- **Petitioner registration.** A parallel commission
  (`docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md`) is in flight and
  will define how a petitioner declares itself to the Reckoner — the
  registration contract, the per-source policy block, the source-id
  validator. This Reckonings doc names the auto-wired event surface
  registration will plug into (`book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
  with the registered petitioner's standing order filtering on
  `outcome` and `source`), but does **not** specify the registration
  shape. When the registration commission lands, the per-source
  filtering convention should be cross-referenced from here.

- **Retention trip-wires.** v0 ships append-only forever with the
  storage-growth math above. The trip-wires that warrant a future
  retention-design commission are concrete:
  1. A Sentinel or Laboratory archival sink lands and an operator
     wants to point an archival relay at it.
  2. An operator complains about Reckonings book size or about
     Stacks query latency on the per-petition timeline query.
  3. Six months of production-growth measurement validates the
     5.26M rows / year projection (or, more interestingly, refutes
     it by 2× or more in either direction).
  Until one of those fires, retention is "do nothing." Designing the
  archive shape before it has a pull is premature; the future
  archival pattern in the Retention section is the placeholder, not
  the design.

- **Queue-observer fold-back.** The existing
  `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` is a narrow CDC observer that
  emits `reckoner.writ-stuck` / `reckoner.writ-failed` /
  `reckoner.queue-drained` Lattice pulses (see
  [apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md)). It was renamed
  out of the `reckoner` plugin id so that id can be reused for the
  new petition-scheduling Reckoner. An open question is whether the
  queue-observer's responsibilities (writ-lifecycle observation
  feeding Lattice pulses) fold back into the new Reckoner core, or
  stay in the renamed sibling. The Reckonings book is unaffected either way —
  the queue-observer's pulses go to the Lattice, not to the
  Reckonings book — but the conceptual seam between "petition
  evaluation" and "writ-lifecycle observation" wants to be settled
  before the new Reckoner core ships.

- **When named events earn their keep.** D8 rejects a named-events
  emission surface (`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, etc.)
  on the grounds that auto-wiring + a one-line in-relay outcome
  filter serves every consumer auto-wiring can serve. The conditions
  under which named events earn their keep:
  1. A non-Clockworks consumer of the Reckonings stream appears —
     e.g. an external webhook bus, a Discord channel, an HTTP
     subscriber — that cannot conveniently consume the framework's
     `ChangeEvent<T>` shape and would benefit from a named event
     with a flat payload.
  2. A measurable performance penalty appears in the in-relay filter
     path. Today the filter is one branch on a tagged union; if a
     future profiling pass shows the change-event payload is large
     enough that all-relays-receive-all-events-and-filter is
     measurably worse than per-outcome dispatch, the surface
     justifies its own emit path.
  3. The validator's reserved-namespace policy gains teeth on
     `book.*` events and the same pass extends teeth to `reckoning.*`.
  Until at least one of those fires, the auto-wired single channel
  remains sufficient.

---

## See Also

- [docs/architecture/clockworks.md](clockworks.md) — the CDC
  auto-wiring contract and the reserved-namespace policy.
- [docs/architecture/apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md) —
