- session: cdd4c6ee-e5d7-4369-8b0e-121521a88243
  date: 2026-04-28
  item: "Diagnosed seal failure for rig-mof48r14-2b7e69f0 (Apr-26 reckoner-rename writ w-moera3n9). Initial framing was concurrent-push contention (existing c-mof5scdo hypothesis) but the worktree HEAD reflog at .nexus/codexes/nexus.git/worktrees/draft-mof48rjm-edd7a286/logs/HEAD told a different story: two `git checkout HEAD~1` events between the implement commit (381e5b4) and the seal start (02:22:15Z) had detached HEAD from the branch. The rebase that followed produced 1c15a36 at detached HEAD but did not advance refs/heads/draft-mof48rjm-edd7a286 (still 381e5b4); each retry iteration re-resolved the stale branch ref, FF kept failing, loop exited with the misleading 'Sealing failed after 3 retries.' Seal had no time for genuine concurrent contention either — engine ran in 3 seconds. Confirmed the work itself landed on origin/main as 1c15a36 via Sean's manual recovery on Apr-26 (per coco-log session 003e859f). Cleaned up the orphaned draft via `nsg draft abandon --force` (worktree directory + bare-clone branch ref both gone, `git worktree list` clean). Opened c-mohzb1ha under c-mof5scdo capturing the detached-HEAD root cause and the reattach-not-fail decision. Posted draft commission w-mohzif1s 'Reattach detached-HEAD branch ref in the seal flow' (codex=nexus, complexity=3) — fix scoped to the rebase retry path: after each rebase, if source branch ref doesn't match worktree HEAD, update branch ref to HEAD and continue; reattach is logged so the failure mode stays observable. Out-of-scope fences: no detached-HEAD prevention in implement/review/revise engines, no retry-budget rework, no advanceToRemote changes. Sean confirmed mechanism (reattach not fail) and complexity. Brief drafted in .scratch/ for offline review then deleted post-dispatch."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 5a7ee279-6433-4b84-ade8-6fd109d1c4a2
  date: 2026-04-28
  item: "Reviewed docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md (the Apr-26 commission output, w-moera26f / commit 58083c7) and revised it interactively with Sean. Initial review surfaced seven probes — four of which Sean approved as straight tightenings (urgency indexing now that priority is the Reckoner's organizing axis; writer-enforced-invariant note for the iff-outcome discriminated union since Stacks won't validate it; deferCount semantics under no-ops — counter advances only on outcome:'deferred' rows, not on re-evaluations of held petitions; forward-link the wake-up dispatch path to Reckoner-core for `deferSignal` → standing order and `deferUntil` → schedule). Fifth probe was a substantive design conversation: Sean pushed back on the doc's 'every petition produces a row every tick' assumption — 'are they useful? is it literally every petition gets one every tick? I think I was initially assuming the reckoner wouldn't look at everything every tick.' Pressure-tested the no-op design and surfaced two conflated concerns: (a) the journal schema (this doc) and (b) the Reckoner's per-tick scoping policy (which petitions get weighed per tick — Reckoner-core, separate). Reframed the journal from tick-shaped to event-shaped logging: the Reckoner writes a row only when consideration produced a state transition or substantive re-evaluation (e.g. wake-up signal fired and re-weighed). Ticks where the Reckoner doesn't reach a petition, or re-encounters unchanged conditions, produce no row — absence-of-row becomes the load-bearing signal. Decoupled scoping policy from journal schema; schema accommodates either sweep-everything or priority-bounded walk. Redid storage-growth math: ~525K-1.5M rows/year (~250-800 MB/year) at steady state, roughly 10× below the earlier projection. Opened c-moi1tf8t under c-mod99ris (Reckoner design root) for the Reckoner-core scoping-policy commission. Committed as nexus 5a99398 (docs commit only — staged just docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md from a working tree with unrelated mods to lattice/sentinel/tools)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

1	# The Reckonings Book — Schema & CDC Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft**
4	
5	Owner plugin: `reckoner` · Book name: `reckonings` · Sibling docs:
6	[clockworks.md](clockworks.md), [apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md),
7	[apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md),
8	[apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md),
9	[apparatus/sentinel.md](apparatus/sentinel.md).
10	
11	> **⚠️ Forward-looking design.** This document specifies the
12	> Reckoner's evaluation-log book before the Reckoner core itself is
13	> commissioned. The plugin id `reckoner` and the existing
14	> `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` (formerly `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus`;
15	> a narrow queue-observer that emits Lattice pulses for `writ-stuck` /
16	> `writ-failed` / `queue-drained`) have been renamed in a separate
17	> commission so this id can be reused for the new petition-scheduling
18	> Reckoner. Nothing in `packages/plugins/sentinel/` is touched by this
19	> design — see Open Questions for the integration.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Intent
24	
25	The Reckonings book is the Reckoner's **evaluation journal**: one
26	append-only row per meaningful consideration, persisted to The Stacks
27	as `stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')`. The journal is
28	**event-shaped, not tick-shaped** — the Reckoner writes a row when a
29	consideration produced a state transition (accept / defer / decline)
30	or when a re-evaluation was substantive enough to record (e.g. a
31	wake-up signal fired and the Reckoner re-weighed against current
32	conditions). Ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition, or
33	re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
34	produce no row.
35	
36	This means **absence-of-row is itself a signal**: a patron, a
37	downstream petitioner, or a future ethnographer asking "what has the
38	Reckoner decided about this petition?" gets the answer from the rows
39	that exist; the lack of any row for a petition since timestamp T means
40	"the Reckoner has not reached it yet, or no condition has changed
41	that warranted re-evaluation."
42	
43	*Which* petitions get weighed on a given tick — and the per-tick
44	budget that bounds how far down the priority order the Reckoner walks
45	— is a Reckoner-core scoping-policy decision, not a journal-schema
46	decision, and lives in the Reckoner-core commission. The schema here
47	accommodates either a sweep-everything-every-tick policy or a
48	priority-bounded walk; the journal records what was considered, not
49	the policy that picked it.
50	
51	This doc settles the schema, the index set, the retention stance, the
52	CDC attachment, and the conceptual framing relative to the
53	petition-state book that lives next door. It is decision-supporting
54	prose only — downstream Reckoner-core implementers consume this design
55	as a settled contract; no code, schema declaration, plugin manifest, or
56	`guild.json` block ships from this commission.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Book Identity
61	
62	`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')` — `ownerId` is `reckoner`, the
63	plugin id of the new Reckoner-core; book name is the plural noun
64	`reckonings`, matching the sibling event-log naming convention
65	(`events`, `pulses`, `sessions`, `transcripts`).
66	
67	The auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks event surface for this book is
68	therefore `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
69	`book.reckoner.reckonings.updated`, and
70	`book.reckoner.reckonings.deleted`. Of these only the `.created` event
71	ever fires in practice — see the Immutability section below — and that
72	single event is the channel petitioners subscribe to (see CDC
73	Attachment).
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Record Schema
78	
79	### Outcome enum
80	
81	Every Reckonings record carries a four-state `outcome`:
82	
83	```
84	'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op'
85	```
86	
87	- **accepted** — the Reckoner created a writ for the petition and
88	  flipped the petition state to `accepted`. The resulting writ flows
89	  into the standard rig pipeline (Distiller → Sage → Artificer), same
90	  as a patron-posted commission.
91	- **deferred** — the Reckoner held the petition until a wake-up signal
92	  fires, with structured reason metadata (see below).
93	- **declined** — the Reckoner rejected the petition on validity
94	  grounds. The decline scope is intentionally narrow (see Decline
95	  Reasons) — "this work isn't worth doing on merit" is patron
96	  territory and produces a `withdrawn` transition by the patron, not
97	  a Reckoner-issued decline.
98	- **no-op** — the Reckoner re-weighed the petition this tick against
99	  changed conditions and chose to hold without a state transition.
100	  Typical examples: a deferred petition's wake-up signal fired and the
101	  Reckoner re-evaluated but still held; a sibling resolution shifted
102	  the priority queue and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's
103	  position; capacity changed and the Reckoner reconsidered. A `no-op`
104	  row records that the Reckoner did real work — not the heartbeat of
105	  the polling loop. See No-op Handling for the absence-of-row
106	  convention that distinguishes "we weighed and held" from "we haven't
107	  reached this yet."
108	
109	`withdrawn` is a petitioner-initiated transition and is **not** a
110	Reckoner-consideration outcome, so it does not produce a Reckonings
111	row.
112	
113	### Petition shape (settled upstream)
114	
115	The Reckoner reads petitions from the petition-state book; the schema
116	of that book is settled by an adjacent commission and reproduced here
117	so this doc is self-contained. Each petition carries:
118	
119	- `source` — petitioner identity (vision-keeper, future tech-debt
120	  watchers, the laboratory introspection writer, ad-hoc patron
121	  petitions, …).
122	- `intent` — short imperative sentence describing the work the
123	  petitioner wants done.
124	- `rationale` — long-form prose justifying the petition.
125	- `priority_signals` — a structured block: `urgency` (an enum,
126	  `immediate | urgent | normal | low`) plus an optional
127	  `strategic_ref` pointing into a vision-document or roadmap entry.
128	- `context_anchors` — file paths, writ ids, transcript ids, and
129	  similar handles a downstream specifier can use as starting points.
130	
131	There is no pre-built brief or spec on a petition. Specification work
132	happens **after** acceptance through the existing Distiller → Sage
133	pipeline, the same path patron-posted commissions take.
134	
135	### Petition lifecycle (settled upstream)
136	
137	```
138	new → pending → { accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn }
139	```
140	
141	- `accepted`, `declined`, and `withdrawn` are terminal.
142	- `deferred → pending` re-awakens on a wake-up signal.
143	- `withdrawn` is petitioner-initiated and produces **no** Reckonings
144	  row — the Reckoner never weighed it as part of its own
145	  consideration loop.
146	
147	### Record body
148	
149	```typescript
150	// Illustrative — declared in the Reckoner-core commission, not here.
151	interface ReckoningDoc {
152	  /** Unique id (`rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`). Sortable by creation time. */
153	  id: string;
154	
155	  /** The petition this record is about. */
156	  petitionId: string;
157	
158	  /** Lean denormalized projection — see "Lean snapshot" below. */
159	  source: string;
160	  urgency: 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';
161	
162	  /** Outcome enum — drives the discriminated-union reason fields. */
163	  outcome: 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';
164	
165	  /**
166	   * Triggering Clockworks event id — the `clockworks.timer` row that
167	   * produced this tick. Optional only on the patron fast path.
168	   * See "Tick identity".
169	   */
170	  tickEventId?: string;
171	
172	  /** ISO timestamp when the Reckoner completed this consideration. */
173	  consideredAt: string;
174	
175	  // ── Outcome-keyed reason metadata (flat optionals) ──────────────
176	  // Consumer types should encode the iff-outcome invariant via a
177	  // discriminated union; the persisted shape stays flat so indexes
178	  // can name top-level fields directly.
179	
180	  /** outcome === 'declined' */
181	  declineReason?:
182	    | 'malformed'

... [783 lines truncated] ...

diff --cc docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md
index 42c6c27,69751cd..0000000
--- a/docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md
+++ b/docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md
@@@ -354,22 -405,55 +405,67 @@@ A row with `outcome: 'accepted'` carrie
  
  ## No-op Handling
  
- **Every consideration produces a row, including no-ops.** The
- Reckoner does not silently drop a tick where it weighed a petition
- without producing a transition.
+ A `no-op` row records that **the Reckoner did real evaluative work
+ that did not produce a state transition**. It is not the heartbeat of
+ the polling loop — ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition,
+ or re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
+ produce no row at all.
  
++<<<<<<< HEAD
 +The single-audit-trail-truth argument is the rationale: a petitioner
 +asking "did the Reckoner ever look at me?" should get the answer from
 +one query against the Reckonings book, not from a join across the
 +petition-state book and the events book and a heuristic. Splitting
 +the journal into "considered with state change" and "considered
 +without state change" — keeping the second set in some sibling
 +overlay or reconstructing it from the clockworks.timer stream — would
 +duplicate the journaling work, complicate every consumer query, and
 +push reasoning about completeness onto callers.
- 
- The journal is the audit trail; the petition-state book is not asked
- to double as event journal.
++=======
+ ### When a no-op row is written
++>>>>>>> 5a99398 (docs(reckonings-book): event-shaped journal + four design tightenings)
+ 
+ The Reckoner writes a `no-op` row when a consideration was
+ *substantive* but the conclusion was still "hold." The canonical
+ cases:
+ 
+ - A deferred petition's `deferSignal` fired (its wake-up event
+   pattern matched). The Reckoner re-weighed against current state
+   and chose to keep holding rather than transition. The wake-up was
+   meaningful even though the outcome did not change.
+ - A deferred petition's `deferUntil` deadline passed and the
+   Reckoner's scheduled re-tick re-evaluated it. Same logic — the
+   re-evaluation was real work, recorded, even if the conclusion did
+   not transition.
+ - A pending petition was re-weighed because a sibling resolved
+   (acceptance or withdrawal shifted the priority queue) or capacity
+   changed, and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's standing.
+   The re-evaluation was triggered by a change in conditions, not by
+   the tick itself.
+ 
+ ### When a row is **not** written
+ 
+ - The Reckoner did not reach the petition this tick (priority-bounded
+   walk stopped before it). No row.
+ - The Reckoner ticked, encountered the petition, but no condition had
+   changed since the last consideration — same priority order, same
+   capacity, same wake-up state. No row. The prior conclusion stands
+   by inheritance.
+ 
+ ### Absence-of-row is the signal
+ 
+ The "did anyone look at me?" question is answered by **any** row
+ existing for the petition. A petition with no rows has not yet been
+ reached or has had no condition change warrant re-evaluation; that is
+ itself meaningful information. A petition with a recent terminal row
+ (`accepted` / `declined`) has its outcome on file. A petition with a
+ deferred row and several no-op rows has been actively re-weighed
+ multiple times since the last transition.
+ 
+ This convention shifts a small amount of reasoning onto callers ("no
+ row" means a specific thing) in exchange for an event-shaped journal
+ that scales with decision events rather than with tick frequency. The
+ storage-growth math (next section) reflects this design.
  
  ### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records

404	---
405	
406	## No-op Handling
407	
408	A `no-op` row records that **the Reckoner did real evaluative work
409	that did not produce a state transition**. It is not the heartbeat of
410	the polling loop — ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition,
411	or re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
412	produce no row at all.
413	
414	<<<<<<< HEAD
415	The single-audit-trail-truth argument is the rationale: a petitioner
416	asking "did the Reckoner ever look at me?" should get the answer from
417	one query against the Reckonings book, not from a join across the
418	petition-state book and the events book and a heuristic. Splitting
419	the journal into "considered with state change" and "considered
420	without state change" — keeping the second set in some sibling
421	overlay or reconstructing it from the clockworks.timer stream — would
422	duplicate the journaling work, complicate every consumer query, and
423	push reasoning about completeness onto callers.
424	=======
425	### When a no-op row is written
426	>>>>>>> 5a99398 (docs(reckonings-book): event-shaped journal + four design tightenings)
427	
428	The Reckoner writes a `no-op` row when a consideration was
429	*substantive* but the conclusion was still "hold." The canonical
430	cases:
431	
432	- A deferred petition's `deferSignal` fired (its wake-up event
433	  pattern matched). The Reckoner re-weighed against current state
434	  and chose to keep holding rather than transition. The wake-up was
435	  meaningful even though the outcome did not change.
436	- A deferred petition's `deferUntil` deadline passed and the
437	  Reckoner's scheduled re-tick re-evaluated it. Same logic — the
438	  re-evaluation was real work, recorded, even if the conclusion did
439	  not transition.
440	- A pending petition was re-weighed because a sibling resolved
441	  (acceptance or withdrawal shifted the priority queue) or capacity
442	  changed, and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's standing.
443	  The re-evaluation was triggered by a change in conditions, not by
444	  the tick itself.
445	
446	### When a row is **not** written
447	
448	- The Reckoner did not reach the petition this tick (priority-bounded
449	  walk stopped before it). No row.
450	- The Reckoner ticked, encountered the petition, but no condition had
451	  changed since the last consideration — same priority order, same
452	  capacity, same wake-up state. No row. The prior conclusion stands
453	  by inheritance.
454	
455	### Absence-of-row is the signal
456	
457	The "did anyone look at me?" question is answered by **any** row
458	existing for the petition. A petition with no rows has not yet been
459	reached or has had no condition change warrant re-evaluation; that is
460	itself meaningful information. A petition with a recent terminal row
461	(`accepted` / `declined`) has its outcome on file. A petition with a
462	deferred row and several no-op rows has been actively re-weighed
463	multiple times since the last transition.
464	
465	This convention shifts a small amount of reasoning onto callers ("no
466	row" means a specific thing) in exchange for an event-shaped journal
467	that scales with decision events rather than with tick frequency. The
468	storage-growth math (next section) reflects this design.
469	
470	### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records
471	
472	When a `no-op` row *is* written, it carries `source` and `urgency`
473	exactly like every other outcome — same lean snapshot, same tick
474	stamp, same `consideredAt`. The modest byte savings of a stripped
475	no-op shape don't justify branching the read path for every consumer
476	query: filters like "since T, all considerations of urgent petitions"
477	must work uniformly across all four outcomes, and the indexes that
478	support those filters need every row to be the same shape.

file.content:
`consideredAt` only in the patron-fast-path case where the
  acceptance happens outside a tick. In the normal case the two are
  identical.

---

## No-op Handling

A `no-op` row records that **the Reckoner did real evaluative work
that did not produce a state transition**. It is not the heartbeat of
the polling loop — ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition,
or re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
produce no row at all.

<<<<<<< HEAD
The single-audit-trail-truth argument is the rationale: a petitioner
asking "did the Reckoner ever look at me?" should get the answer from
one query against the Reckonings book, not from a join across the
petition-state book and the events book and a heuristic. Splitting
the journal into "considered with state change" and "considered
without state change" — keeping the second set in some sibling
overlay or reconstructing it from the clockworks.timer stream — would
duplicate the journaling work, complicate every consumer query, and
push reasoning about completeness onto callers.
=======
### When a no-op row is written
>>>>>>> 5a99398 (docs(reckonings-book): event-shaped journal + four design tightenings)

The Reckoner writes a `no-op` row when a consideration was
*substantive* but the conclusion was still "hold." The canonical
cases:

- A deferred petition's `deferSignal` fired (its wake-up event
  pattern matched). The Reckoner re-weighed against current state
  and chose to keep holding rather than transition. The wake-up was
  meaningful even though the outcome did not change.
- A deferred petition's `deferUntil` deadline passed and the
  Reckoner's scheduled re-tick re-evaluated it. Same logic — the
  re-evaluation was real work, recorded, even if the conclusion did
  not transition.
- A pending petition was re-weighed because a sibling resolved
  (acceptance or withdrawal shifted the priority queue) or capacity
  changed, and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's standing.
  The re-evaluation was triggered by a change in conditions, not by
  the tick itself.

### When a row is **not** written

- The Reckoner did not reach the petition this tick (priority-bounded
  walk stopped before it). No row.
- The Reckoner ticked, encountered the petition, but no condition had
  changed since the last consideration — same priority order, same
  capacity, same wake-up state. No row. The prior conclusion stands
  by inheritance.

### Absence-of-row is the signal

The "did anyone look at me?" question is answered by **any** row
existing for the petition. A petition with no rows has not yet been
reached or has had no condition change warrant re-evaluation; that is
itself meaningful information. A petition with a recent terminal row
(`accepted` / `declined`) has its outcome on file. A petition with a
deferred row and several no-op rows has been actively re-weighed
multiple times since the last transition.

This convention shifts a small amount of reasoning onto callers ("no
row" means a specific thing) in exchange for an event-shaped journal
that scales with decision events rather than with tick frequency. The
storage-growth math (next section) reflects this design.

### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records

When a `no-op` row *is* written, it carries `source` and `urgency`
exactly like every other outcome — same lean snapshot, same tick
stamp, same `consideredAt`. The modest byte savings of a stripped
no-op shape don't justify branching the read path for every consumer
query: filters like "since T, all considerations of urgent petitions"
must work uniformly across all four outcomes, and the indexes that
support those filters need every row to be the same shape.

content.text:
Done. Both repos pushed.

## Summary

**Doc changes** (`nexus 187a62c`):

- **Reframed journal as event-shaped, not tick-shaped** — Reckoner writes a row only on state transitions or substantive re-evaluations; ticks that don't reach a petition or re-encounter unchanged conditions produce no row. Absence-of-row becomes the load-bearing signal.
- **Decoupled scoping policy from journal schema** — *which* petitions get weighed per tick is now explicitly a Reckoner-core decision, not a Reckonings-book commitment. Schema accommodates either sweep-everything or priority-bounded walk.
- **Storage-growth math redone** — ~525K–1.5M rows/year (~250–800 MB/year) at steady state, roughly 10× below the earlier projection. Trip-wires updated.
- **`urgency` and `['urgency', 'consideredAt']` indexed** — priority is the Reckoner's organizing axis.
- **Writer-enforced invariant** — explicit note that the iff-outcome discriminated union is enforced by the Reckoner core, not the Stacks schema.
- **`deferCount` semantics clarified** — counter advances only on `outcome: 'deferred'` rows, not on no-op re-evaluations of held petitions.
- **Wake-up dispatch forward-link** — `deferSignal` → standing order, `deferUntil` → `schedule:` order, mechanism owned by Reckoner-core.
- **Indexes block disclaimer** added to match the schema block.

**Click**: opened c-moi1tf8t under c-mod99ris for the Reckoner-core scoping policy commission.

**Rebase note**: resolved a single conflict in the No-op Handling section — origin/main had renamed `schedule.fired → clockworks.timer` (events-kit migration) and added an `apparatus/sentinel.md` reference. Took the rename through, dropped the now-obsolete "single-audit-trail-truth" paragraph since the new framing supersedes it.

content: Reviewed and revised the Reckonings book design with you — reframed the journal as event-shaped (absence-of-row is the signal), added urgency indexing and three other tightenings, opened a click for the Reckoner-core scoping policy, and pushed both repos. Next: nothing pending; awaiting your direction. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: ok, great. now review the `/workspace/nexus-mk2/.scratch/reckoner/reckoner-contract.md` document. This describes the latest plan for implementing the reckoner. Is there anything in this version which contradicts our plan for reckonings-book?

content.content:
1	# Reckoner Contract — Held Writs and Dimension-Driven Scheduling
2	
3	Status: **Draft** (alternative to `petitioner-registration.md`)
4	
5	> **⚠️ v0 scope.** This document fixes the contract between the
6	> (forthcoming) petition-scheduler Reckoner and the rest of the
7	> framework. v0 covers: the petition data shape (priority
8	> dimensions, complexity, rationale, payload, labels), the
9	> kit-static petitioner registry, the convention for opting a writ
10	> into Reckoner gating (`writ.ext['reckoner']`), the Reckoner's
11	> behavioral contract, the optional `reckoner.petition()` helper,
12	> the `enforceRegistration` config flag, the Reckonings evaluation
13	> log, and the trust model. The Reckoner's combination function
14	> (how dimensions become scheduling weight), the Reckonings book
15	> schema beyond the evaluation-log shape, the patron-emit surface
16	> (CLI / MCP), and any non-`vision-keeper` petitioner are
17	> explicitly out of scope.
18	
19	> **Reframing note.** This draft is an alternative shape for the
20	> petitioner-registration contract. It reframes the problem:
21	> instead of a separate "petition" entity that materializes into a
22	> writ on approval, a petition IS a writ — in `new` phase, carrying
23	> Reckoner ext. The Reckoner is one authority among several that
24	> can transition writs from `new` to an active phase. Side-by-side
25	> comparison to the original framing is in §13.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Dependencies
30	
31	```
32	contract spans: ['clerk', 'spider', 'stacks', 'clockworks']
33	```
34	
35	- **The [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md)** — the writ tracker. Held
36	  writs in `new` phase are normal Clerk writs; the Reckoner uses
37	  Clerk's transition API to approve or decline them. Clerk knows
38	  nothing about the Reckoner; the dependency is one-way.
39	- **The [Spider](apparatus/spider.md)** — the dispatcher. Spider
40	  doesn't dispatch writs in `new` phase. Once the Reckoner
41	  transitions a writ to `open` (or the type-equivalent active
42	  state), Spider picks it up and dispatches via the writ type's
43	  rig template.
44	- **[Stacks](apparatus/stacks.md)** — the storage substrate. The
45	  Reckoner watches CDC events on the writs book to find writs
46	  needing consideration.
47	- **[Clockworks](clockworks.md)** — the event substrate. The auto-
48	  wired `book.clerk.writs.{created,updated}` events feed the
49	  Reckoner's CDC handler.
50	
51	---
52	
53	## 1. The mechanism
54	
55	### Posting a Reckoner-gated writ (Workflow 1 — direct)
56	
57	A petitioner posts a writ in the standard way, with one addition:
58	the writ carries `writ.ext['reckoner']` with at minimum `source`
59	and `priority`. That ext is the signal to the Reckoner that this
60	writ requires its consideration.
61	
62	```typescript
63	await clerk.post({
64	  type:     'mandate',
65	  title:    'Address vision drift detected at 04:00 UTC',
66	  body:     '...',
67	  codex:    'nexus',
68	  parentId: 'w-...',
69	  ext: {
70	    reckoner: {
71	      source:    'vision-keeper.snapshot',
72	      priority: {
73	        visionRelation: 'vision-violator',
74	        severity:       'serious',
75	        scope:          'major-area',
76	        time:           { decay: true, deadline: null },
77	        domain:         ['quality'],
78	      },
79	      complexity: 'bounded',
80	      rationale:  'Drift detected on snapshot equality check; '
81	                + 'serious because seal step has dropped commits '
82	                + '5+ times daily this week.',
83	      payload:    { /* opaque petitioner-defined data */ },
84	    },
85	  },
86	});
87	```
88	
89	The writ exists in `new` phase (Clerk's default for new writs).
90	Spider doesn't dispatch from `new`. The Reckoner observes CDC,
91	sees `writ.ext['reckoner']`, validates the source against its
92	registry (see §5), and adopts responsibility for transitioning the
93	writ.
94	
95	This workflow is always available — any apparatus that can call
96	`clerk.post()` (which is to say, any loaded apparatus) can post a
97	Reckoner-gated writ this way. No runtime dependency on the
98	Reckoner API.
99	
100	### `reckoner.petition()` helper (Workflow 2 — canonical)
101	
102	For petitioner ergonomics, the Reckoner exposes a thin wrapper
103	that constructs the writ in the correct shape:
104	
105	```typescript
106	interface ReckonerApi {
107	  /**
108	   * Post a writ in `new` phase with Reckoner ext set correctly.
109	   * Validates dimension values against the schema, applies
110	   * defaults for omitted dimensions, validates source against
111	   * the registry, and calls clerk.post() under the hood.
112	   *
113	   * Equivalent in effect to calling clerk.post() with manual ext
114	   * construction (Workflow 1); this helper exists for type
115	   * checking, default handling, and discoverability.
116	   */
117	  petition(request: PetitionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
118	
119	  /**
120	   * Withdraw a held writ by transitioning it to cancelled.
121	   * Convenience wrapper around clerk.transition(); identical
122	   * effect to calling Clerk directly.
123	   */
124	  withdraw(writId: string, reason?: string): Promise<WritDoc>;
125	}
126	
127	interface PetitionRequest {
128	  // writ fields (passed through to clerk.post)
129	  type:      string;
130	  title:     string;
131	  body:      string;
132	  codex:     string;
133	  parentId?: string;
134	
135	  // ext.reckoner fields
136	  source:      string;                          // required, registered
137	  priority:    Priority;                        // see §3
138	  complexity?: ComplexityTier;                  // see §4
139	  rationale?:  string;                          // see §2
140	  payload?:    unknown;                         // opaque
141	  labels?:     Record<string, string>;          // additive metadata
142	}
143	```
144	
145	The helper is the **canonical** path for ergonomic petitioner
146	code: a single call, type-checked dimensions, defaults applied,
147	registry validated at API-call time. Petitioners that prefer
148	direct posting (Workflow 1) get identical Reckoner behavior; the
149	helper is a convenience layer, not a gate.
150	
151	There is **no Workflow 3** (post-then-petition). Adding ext to an
152	already-posted writ via a separate `reckoner.petition(writId, ...)`
153	call introduces atomicity concerns (orphaned writs in `new` with
154	no ext) and serves no current use case. If retroactive gating
155	becomes needed later, adding a `petition(writId, ...)` overload is
156	purely additive.
157	
158	### Reckoner behavior
159	
160	The Reckoner subscribes to the auto-wired `book.clerk.writs`
161	events. For each writ event, it inspects the writ:
162	
163	1. If the writ is **not** in `new` phase, ignore.
164	2. If the writ does **not** have `ext['reckoner']`, ignore — this
165	   is not a Reckoner-gated writ; some other authority owns the
166	   transition.
167	3. **Source check.** If the writ's `ext['reckoner'].source` is
168	   **not** registered (see §5):
169	   - When `enforceRegistration` is true (default): decline the
170	     writ, transitioning `new` → `cancelled` with a reason
171	     naming the unknown source.
172	   - When `enforceRegistration` is false: log a warning and
173	     proceed.
174	4. **Per-source ops controls.** If the source is registered but
175	   currently disabled by Reckoner config, decline the writ with a
176	   reason naming the disable reason.
177	5. Otherwise the writ is a held petition. The Reckoner evaluates
178	   its priority dimensions and complexity against current state
179	   and decides:
180	   - **Approve** → transition `new` → `open` (or the type's
181	     equivalent active state). Spider then picks up.
182	   - **Defer** → leave in `new`. Append a reckoning entry to the
183	     Reckonings book noting the deferral. Re-evaluate on the
184	     next scheduling tick or the next state-relevant event.
185	   - **Decline** → transition `new` → `cancelled`. The decline
186	     reason is stamped on the writ via the resolution field.
187	
188	The Reckoner is one authority among several that can transition
189	writs out of `new`. Patron manual transitions, planner-driven
190	automatic transitions, and any other authorities continue to
191	operate on writs without Reckoner ext.
192	
193	### Withdrawal
194	
195	The petitioner withdraws a held writ by transitioning it to
196	`cancelled` via Clerk's standard transition API:
197	
198	```typescript
199	await clerk.transition(writId, 'cancelled', {
200	  reason: 'Snapshot superseded by drift detected before this ran.',
201	});
202	```
203	
204	The Reckoner's `withdraw(writId, reason?)` helper is a thin
205	wrapper around the same call.
206	
207	If withdrawal lands while the writ is still in `new` (the common
208	case), the Reckoner's CDC handler stops considering it. If
209	withdrawal lands after approval (writ already in `open` or
210	further), it follows the normal lifecycle for cancelling
211	in-flight work — same as any other writ being cancelled mid-
212	execution. No special Reckoner logic required.
213	
214	### After approval
215	
216	Once approved, the writ is a normal active writ. Spider
217	dispatches; the appropriate rig runs; engines do the work;
218	lifecycle proceeds normally. The Reckoner's involvement ends at
219	approval.
220	
221	The data the petitioner attached in `writ.ext['reckoner'].payload`
222	is available to the running engine via standard writ access — the
223	engine reads it if it cares. Most rigs won't.
224	
225	For animas working the writ, no special tools are needed —
226	everything they need is on the writ. If structured payload
227	inspection becomes common, the Reckoner can ship a small read-
228	tool that pretty-prints `writ.ext['reckoner']`, but it's not
229	required for correctness.
230	
231	---
232	
233	## 2. The petition shape
234	
235	The full `writ.ext['reckoner']` shape:
236	
237	```typescript
238	interface ReckonerExt {
239	  /**
240	   * Required. Identifies the petitioner. Must match a registered
241	   * petitioner descriptor (see §5) when enforceRegistration is on.
242	   */
243	  source: string;
244	
245	  /**
246	   * Required. Multi-dimensional priority. See §3.
247	   */
248	  priority: Priority;
249	
250	  /**
251	   * Optional petitioner-side coarse cost estimate. Refined by the
252	   * Astrolabe at plan time. See §4.
253	   */
254	  complexity?: ComplexityTier;
255	
256	  /**
257	   * Optional free-form justification for the priority claim.
258	   * Distinct from writ.body (which describes the work).
259	   * Documents why the dimensions are claimed at the levels stated.
260	   * Useful for code review, audit, and observability of
261	   * mis-claims.
262	   */
263	  rationale?: string;
264	
265	  /**
266	   * Opaque petitioner-defined structured data. The Reckoner
267	   * stores it but does not introspect; the rig's implementation
268	   * engine reads it if needed.
269	   */
270	  payload?: unknown;
271	
272	  /**
273	   * Additive non-priority metadata. Multi-instance discrimination
274	   * (e.g. `'vision-keeper.io/vision-id': 'nexus'`),
275	   * observability hints, diagnostic tags. See §11.
276	   */
277	  labels?: Record<string, string>;
278	}
279	```
280	
281	The split between `priority` (how much does this matter?),
282	`complexity` (what does it cost?), and `rationale` (why is this
283	claim warranted?) is intentional — these are conceptually
284	distinct axes that the Reckoner consumes for different purposes.
285	
286	---
287	
288	## 3. Priority dimensions
289	
290	The `priority` field is a structured object across five
291	dimensions. Designed to be **inspectable and defensible** rather
292	than mechanically objective — petitioners declare honest inputs,
293	the Reckoner does judgment-laden contextual collapse. The shared
294	vocabulary makes disagreements visible and resolvable in review.
295	
296	```typescript
297	type Priority = {
298	  visionRelation:
299	    | 'vision-blocker'
300	    | 'vision-violator'
301	    | 'vision-advancer'
302	    | 'vision-neutral';
303	  severity:
304	    | 'critical' | 'serious' | 'moderate' | 'minor';
305	  scope:
306	    | 'whole-product' | 'major-area' | 'minor-area';
307	  time: {
308	    decay:    boolean;
309	    deadline: string | null;        // ISO date
310	  };
311	  domain: Array<
312	    | 'security' | 'compliance' | 'cost' | 'feature' | 'quality'
313	    | 'infrastructure' | 'documentation' | 'research' | 'ergonomics'
314	  >;
315	};
316	```
317	
318	(Note: `complexity` was previously inside the priority object;
319	it's been lifted to a peer field in `ext.reckoner` because it
320	answers a different question — cost rather than priority.)
321	
322	### `visionRelation`
323	
324	How does this petition relate to the product vision? Names a
325	**relationship type**, not a magnitude.
326	
327	- `vision-blocker` — the vision is unreachable without this;
328	  future-blocking.
329	- `vision-violator` — the current product state actively diverges
330	  from the vision; present-tense degradation.
331	- `vision-advancer` — pushes toward an aspirational, not-yet-
332	  realized aspect of the vision.
333	- `vision-neutral` — doesn't touch the product vision;
334	  operational, hygiene, internal tooling, research instrumentation.
335	
336	### `severity`
337	
338	If this petition is not acted on, how bad is the situation? Pure
339	magnitude axis, independent of `visionRelation`.
340	
341	- `critical` — production breakage, security incident,
342	  irreversible damage accruing, agents fully blocked.
343	- `serious` — significant ongoing degradation; daily workarounds.
344	- `moderate` — noticeable but tolerable.
345	- `minor` — easy to live with; cosmetic, convenience, polish.
346	
347	For `vision-advancer` petitions, severity reads as the value /
348	eagerness of the advancement rather than damage from inaction.
349	Same scale orders correctly; interpretation shifts to fit the
350	relationship type.
351	
352	### `scope`
353	
354	What fraction of the system is affected?
355	
356	- `whole-product` — every user / every commission / every guild.
357	- `major-area` — a major feature or subsystem.
358	- `minor-area` — a small slice, one workflow, one command.
359	
360	### `time`
361	
362	Two genuinely independent axes:
363	
364	- `decay: true` — drift sentinels, security exposure,
365	  accumulating technical debt.
366	- `deadline: <iso-date>` — pledged demos, external commitments.
367	- both — common (e.g. "promised May 15, AND every day broken
368	  costs us users").
369	- neither — defer-friendly work.
370	
371	### `domain`
372	
373	Multi-valued tag set for orthogonal classification. **Not** a
374	priority axis — describes what *kind* of work this is.
375	
376	Used by the Reckoner for filtering, patron-tunable weighting (e.g.
377	weight `security` higher than `cost`), and reporting.
378	
379	### Defaults when omitted
380	
381	```typescript
382	{
383	  visionRelation: 'vision-neutral',
384	  severity:       'minor',
385	  scope:          'minor-area',
386	  time:           { decay: false, deadline: null },
387	  domain:         [],
388	}
389	```
390	
391	The `reckoner.petition()` helper applies these defaults; direct
392	`clerk.post()` callers (Workflow 1) are responsible for supplying
393	priority values, though omitted dimension fields fall back to
394	defaults at consideration time.
395	
396	### Combination semantics
397	
398	The Reckoner does **judgment-laden** collapse — these dimensions
399	are inputs to a contextual scheduling decision, not coordinates
400	in a priority space with a fixed lexicographic order. The
401	combination-function shape (rules / weighted sum / LLM scorer)
402	belongs to the Reckoner-core or Reckonings-book commission, not
403	to this contract.
404	
405	For deterministic fallback ordering when the Reckoner isn't
406	running (manual triage, dry-run inspection), suggested
407	precedence:
408	
409	1. `severity: 'critical'` ahead of everything.
410	2. `visionRelation: 'vision-blocker'` next.
411	3. Then anything with `time.decay: true` or imminent
412	   `time.deadline`.
413	4. Then by severity within remaining.
414	5. Ties broken by scope (broader first), then by `complexity`
415	   (smaller first — quick wins flush before slogs).
416	
417	Suggestion only; the Reckoner is free to weigh differently and
418	patrons can tune.
419	
420	### Deliberate omissions
421	
422	- **Reversibility** — better captured at plan-review time than at
423	  petition-emit time.
424	- **Dependency / unblock-count** — graph property the Reckoner
425	  can compute from the writ tree, not something the petitioner
426	  self-claims.
427	- **A unified urgency scalar** — deliberately not added; the
428	  whole point is to avoid the lossy collapse of the original
429	  4-tier enum.
430	
431	### No allow-list concept
432	
433	There is no per-source priority allow-list. Petitioners declare
434	honest dimension values; mis-claiming is code-review-detectable,
435	parallel to the source-trust model in §6. Authority for the
436	patron-bridge or future security-emergency apparatuses emerges
437	from honestly-claimed dimensions, not from a privileged source
438	name or per-source priority allowance. The petitioner registry
439	(§5) controls *who can petition*, not *what priorities they can
440	claim*.
441	
442	---
443	
444	## 4. Complexity
445	
446	Petitioner-side coarse estimate of expected agent token cost.
447	Refined by the Astrolabe at plan time; this exists only for
448	early-stage trade-offs before plans exist.
449	
450	```typescript
451	type ComplexityTier =
452	  | 'mechanical'
453	  | 'bounded'
454	  | 'exploratory'
455	  | 'open-ended';
456	```
457	
458	- `mechanical` — clear path, minimal exploration. Rename a field,
459	  add a known-shape method, regenerate from a template. Roughly
460	  ~50K–200K tokens.
461	- `bounded` — clear scope, some exploration. Add a feature whose
462	  shape is understood, fix a bug whose location is known,
463	  refactor within one subsystem. Roughly ~200K–1M tokens.
464	- `exploratory` — design judgment required; multiple viable
465	  paths; cross-cutting investigation. Roughly ~1M–5M tokens.
466	- `open-ended` — research, prototyping, or scope likely to grow
467	  under contact with reality. Multi-session work expected.
468	  ~5M+ tokens.
469	- omitted — petitioner has no basis to claim.
470	
471	Token ranges are calibration hints, **not contractual**. They
472	will drift as agent capability changes; periodic re-tuning
473	against actual commission-log data is expected. Petitioners are
474	encouraged to omit rather than guess — omission is honest; a
475	wild guess is misleading.
476	
477	The calibration feedback loop is tracked at `c-mohd0luw`.
478	
479	### Why complexity is separate from priority
480	
481	Priority (§3) answers "how much does this matter?" Complexity
482	answers "what does it cost?" Both feed scheduling, but they're
483	conceptually distinct — a critical-severity petition with
484	mechanical complexity is the kind of thing that should flush
485	quickly; a critical-severity petition with open-ended complexity
486	warrants more careful scheduling. Bundling them obscures the
487	distinction.
488	
489	---
490	
491	## 5. The petitioner registry
492	
493	The Reckoner maintains a kit-static registry of petitioner
494	sources. Petitioners declare themselves via a `petitioners` kit
495	contribution:
496	
497	```typescript
498	// In a plugin's kit (or apparatus's supportKit)
499	export default {
500	  kit: {
501	    requires: ['reckoner'],
502	    petitioners: [
503	      {
504	        source:      'vision-keeper.snapshot',
505	        description: 'Periodic vision-vs-reality snapshots ' +
506	                     'emitted when the keeper observes drift ' +
507	                     'worth surfacing.',
508	      },
509	    ],
510	  },
511	};
512	
513	interface PetitionerDescriptor {
514	  source:      string;
515	  description: string;
516	}
517	```
518	
519	Registration is **kit-static**: the Reckoner consumes the
520	`petitioners` contribution type at boot, builds the registry, and
521	seals it at `phase:started`. Per-plugin-load-cycle framing applies
522	(today equivalent to global seal; positions correctly for future
523	dynamic plugin loading).
524	
525	### Registry purpose
526	
527	The registry is **descriptive infrastructure**, not authority
528	gating:
529	
530	- **Source validation.** Petitions whose `ext.reckoner.source` is
531	  unknown are caught (per §1's source check), with behavior
532	  governed by `enforceRegistration` config (§6).
533	- **Discoverability.** Operators / Oculus can enumerate all
534	  registered petitioner sources without aggregating from
535	  observed petitions.
536	- **Per-source ops controls.** Operators can disable or throttle
537	  specific sources via configuration (e.g., temporarily pause
538	  tech-debt petitions while focusing on a release). See §6.
539	- **Per-source metrics.** The Reckoner can attribute petition
540	  rates, decline rates, and approval rates to named sources.
541	- **Per-source documentation.** The `description` field gives
542	  operators a one-liner about each source.
543	
544	### Kit-vs-kit collision policy
545	
546	Two `petitioners` entries with the same `source` string are a
547	**hard error**. The Reckoner refuses to start with a diagnostic
548	naming both contributing plugin ids and the conflicting source —
549	the winner is never selected by load order. Mirrors the
550	framework-wide collision rule applied to Clerk writ-types,
551	Spider rig-template-mappings, and the Fabricator's engines.
552	
553	### Source-id grammar
554	
555	A source id has the form **`{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`** — the
556	contributing plugin's derived id, a literal `.`, then a kebab-
557	case suffix (lowercase letters, digits, single-hyphen separators;
558	no leading or trailing hyphen). Matches Lattice trigger-types and
559	Clerk link-kinds. Examples:
560	
561	- `vision-keeper.snapshot`
562	- `patron-bridge.commission`
563	- `tech-debt.detected`
564	
565	Malformed source ids hard-fail at startup, never at first emit.
566	
567	### Trust model applies independently
568	
569	The registry does **not** strengthen the trust model. Sources are
570	still emitter-stamped (the petitioner names its own source on
571	each petition); the Reckoner trusts the stamp. The registry adds
572	a layer of "this stamp is recognized" but doesn't prevent
573	mis-stamping in the same way the original handle-based design
574	would have. See §7.
575	
576	---
577	
578	## 6. Configuration
579	
580	The Reckoner reads its configuration from `guild.json` under the
581	`reckoner` key:
582	
583	```json
584	{
585	  "reckoner": {
586	    "enforceRegistration": true,
587	    "disabledSources": []
588	  }
589	}
590	```
591	
592	### `enforceRegistration` (boolean, default `true`)
593	
594	Controls how the Reckoner handles petitions whose
595	`ext.reckoner.source` is not in the registry:
596	
597	- `true` (default) — decline the petition, transitioning the writ
598	  to `cancelled` with a reason naming the unknown source.
599	- `false` — log a warning and proceed with normal consideration.
600	
601	Default is enforce-registration. The opt-out exists for
602	development scenarios (rapid iteration where registering each new
603	petitioner is overhead) and for operational scenarios where a
604	guild wants to be permissive about emergent petitioners.
605	
606	### `disabledSources` (string array, default `[]`)
607	
608	Per-source disable list. Petitions from any source in this array
609	are auto-declined, regardless of registration status. Useful for
610	operational scenarios:
611	
612	- "Pause tech-debt petitions while we focus on a release."
613	- "This petitioner is misbehaving; disable until investigated."
614	- "We're over budget; disable all non-critical petitioners."
615	
616	A future enhancement could expand this to per-source throttling
617	(rate limits) or conditional disabling (disable when X
618	condition); v0 is a simple deny list.
619	
620	### Future config surface
621	
622	This contract reserves the `reckoner` key in `guild.json` for
623	future configuration as the Reckoner-core scheduling logic lands
624	(combination-function tuning, capacity limits, etc.). v0
625	specifies only the two fields above.
626	
627	---
628	
629	## 7. Trust model
630	
631	Petitioners post writs with their dimensions claimed honestly.
632	The Reckoner trusts the claims; mis-claiming is code-review-
633	detectable, parallel to the source-trust model in
634	[Lattice](apparatus/lattice.md).
635	
636	The petitioner registry (§5) is a **descriptive layer**, not an
637	authority gate. A registered source that systematically over-
638	claims its priority dimensions can still get its petitions
639	approved at higher rates than it should — the registry doesn't
640	prevent that. Operators detect mis-claiming via the Reckonings
641	evaluation log (§8) and address it through code review or the
642	`disabledSources` config.
643	
644	Authority for the patron-bridge or future security-emergency
645	apparatuses emerges from honestly-claimed dimensions (e.g.
646	`severity: 'critical', visionRelation: 'vision-violator',
647	domain: ['security']`), not from a privileged source name or
648	registry tier.
649	
650	Mitigations against mis-claiming:
651	
652	- **Code review** by humans and reviewing agents.
653	- **The evaluation log** makes mis-claiming visible
654	  retrospectively — weight applied vs outcome can be analyzed.
655	- **Future apparatuses** (audit, observability) can scan the log
656	  for systematic over-claiming and surface alerts.
657	- **Operational disabling** via `disabledSources` config when
658	  mis-claiming is identified.
659	
660	### Why not handle-based or framework-mediated identity
661	
662	Two alternatives were considered and rejected:
663	
664	- **Handle-based authority** (closures stamp source). Rejected
665	  because the multi-dimensional priority schema eliminated per-
666	  source authority gates entirely — the handle's structural
667	  proof was over-engineering for a failure mode the contract no
668	  longer surfaces.
669	- **Framework-mediated identity** (caller pluginId stamped via
670	  AsyncLocalStorage proxy). Rejected because plugin authors
671	  routinely use raw timer / signal / HTTP / IO callbacks that
672	  drop ALS context (4+ current plugins). Framework-mediated
673	  identity would either fail-loud (breaking deferred work) or
674	  fail-silent (silent authority drift). The identity-proxy work
675	  continues as an independent track (`c-mofxdlwb`) but is not
676	  load-bearing for petitioner authority.
677	
678	---
679	
680	## 8. The Reckonings book
681	
682	The Reckoner owns a `reckoner.reckonings` book — the evaluation
683	log. One record per consideration tick, regardless of whether the
684	consideration produced a state change.
685	
686	Each reckoning captures:
687	
688	- writ id
689	- timestamp
690	- decision (`approve` | `defer` | `decline` | `unchanged` |
691	  `withdrawn`)
692	- reason (free-form string; defer/decline reasons follow
693	  conventions documented by the Reckonings-book commission)
694	- weighed priority at the tick (snapshot of dimensions and
695	  complexity at consideration time, useful when claims change
696	  between ticks)
697	
698	The log is durable; current writ state is the materialized view
699	over accumulated reckonings + the writ's own Clerk lifecycle.
700	
701	The exact record schema is owned by the parallel Reckonings-book
702	commission (`c-modeou1t`). This contract relies only on the
703	existence of an evaluation log; the precise field shapes will
704	track that commission's decisions.
705	
706	---
707	
708	## 9. Feedback receipt
709	
710	A petitioner observes outcomes via standard CDC on the writs
711	book. The auto-wired `book.clerk.writs.updated` event delivers
712	all writ transitions. Three usage patterns:
713	
714	### Channel 1 — event-driven standing order (the canonical path)
715	
716	The petitioner declares a standing order keyed on writ-update
717	events, filtered to writs they posted (typically by source label
718	on the writ's ext, by the writ's parentId, or by other writ-
719	intrinsic attributes).
720	
721	```jsonc
722	{
723	  "clockworks": {
724	    "standingOrders": [
725	      {
726	        "on": "book.clerk.writs.updated",
727	        "run": "vision-keeper-on-decline",
728	        "with": {
729	          "filterExtSource": "vision-keeper.snapshot",
730	          "filterPhase":     "cancelled"
731	        }
732	      }
733	    ]
734	  }
735	}
736	```
737	
738	The relay handler reads the post-commit event payload and reacts.
739	The Reckoner does not duplicate Clockworks's standing-order
740	substrate; the petitioner reuses the vocabulary they already
741	know.
742	
743	(The exact filter shape — `filterExtSource`, etc. — depends on
744	Clockworks's filter capabilities, which are out of scope here.
745	Flagged in §15.)
746	
747	### Channel 2 — polling
748	
749	For periodic reconciliation or "what's outstanding?" snapshots:
750	
751	```typescript
752	const stacks = guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
753	const writs  = stacks.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs');
754	const mine   = await writs.list({
755	  where: [
756	    ['phase', '=', 'new'],
757	    ['ext.reckoner.source', '=', 'vision-keeper.snapshot'],
758	  ],
759	});
760	```
761	
762	(Exact filter syntax follows Stacks's query API.)
763	
764	### Channel 3 — fire-and-forget
765	
766	The petitioner emits and walks away. Re-emission is driven by
767	the petitioner's own conditions; the Reckoner's lifecycle
768	handles dedupe / supersede.
769	
770	### Channel selection
771	
772	Standing order for live reactions; polling for snapshots;
773	fire-and-forget for "I noticed *X*; route it" semantics.
774	
775	---
776	
777	## 10. Lifecycle hooks
778	
779	**v0 declares no hooks.** A petitioner's full surface is the
780	`ext.reckoner` shape (§2) — no `onAccept`, no `canRetry`, no
781	`onDefer`, no callback that runs in the Reckoner's process before
782	a transition.
783	
784	Reasoning: CDC + standing-order observation covers the need
785	without an invocation-ordering contract; pre-empting hooks would
786	introduce ordering questions the framework has no precedent to
787	lean on.
788	
789	Adding a hook later is additive — the Reckoner's API can grow
790	without breaking v0 callers.
791	
792	---
793	
794	## 11. Built-in example: vision-keeper
795	
796	The vision-keeper is the canonical worked example. It exercises
797	every contract surface in this doc:
798	
799	- **Declares its source** in its kit:
800	  ```typescript
801	  petitioners: [{
802	    source:      'vision-keeper.snapshot',
803	    description: 'Vision-vs-reality snapshots emitted when the ' +
804	                 'keeper observes drift worth surfacing.',
805	  }]
806	  ```
807	- **Posts held writs** via `reckoner.petition(...)` (Workflow 2)
808	  when it observes drift or proposes elaboration.
809	- **Stamps source** on each petition (`source:
810	  'vision-keeper.snapshot'`).
811	- **Declares dimensions** matching the situation:
812	  `{ visionRelation: 'vision-violator', severity: 'serious',
813	     scope: 'major-area', time: { decay: true, deadline: null },
814	     domain: ['quality'] }`
815	  for drift-detected snapshots.
816	- **Includes complexity claim** when it has a basis (often
817	  `'bounded'` for known-shape drift remediations; omitted for
818	  open-ended elaborations).
819	- **Includes rationale** explaining why the dimensions are
820	  claimed at the levels stated.
821	- **Attaches payload** in `writ.ext['reckoner'].payload` so the
822	  rig that processes the resulting writ has the full snapshot
823	  available without joining to overlay books.
824	- **Uses labels** to discriminate per-vision instances when
825	  multiple visions are tracked:
826	  `labels: { 'vision-keeper.io/vision-id': 'nexus' }`.
827	- **Withdraws superseded petitions** via `reckoner.withdraw(writId,
828	  reason?)` (or `clerk.transition(writId, 'cancelled', ...)`).
829	- **Observes outcomes** through a Channel-1 standing order on
830	  the writs book filtered to its own source.
831	
832	There is **no patron special-case** in this contract. The
833	patron's emit path (CLI surface, MCP tool, `commission-post`
834	interaction) is owned by a separate **patron-bridge** apparatus
835	that registers as a normal petitioner under
836	`patron-bridge.commission` (or whatever the bridge apparatus
837	chooses) and posts writs like any other petitioner. The patron's
838	authority emerges from the dimensions its petitions claim —
839	typically `severity: 'critical'` and concrete domain values that
840	combine into high scheduling weight — not from a privileged
841	source name.
842	
843	---
844	
845	## 12. Existing precedents
846	
847	This contract composes existing framework primitives rather than
848	inventing new ones:
849	
850	- **The `new` phase** — Clerk's existing pre-dispatch holding
851	  state. Writs in `new` don't dispatch until some authority
852	  transitions them. The Reckoner is one such authority.
853	- **CDC + standing orders** — Clockworks's existing event
854	  substrate. Reckoner watches writ-events; petitioners watch for
855	  outcome-events. No new event mechanism.
856	- **Per-plugin extension slots** — `writ.ext` parallels
857	  `writ.status` (the existing per-plugin slot). Both are
858	  `Record<PluginId, unknown>`; same write-mechanism (a per-plugin
859	  setter API), different semantics:
860	  - `status` = post-hoc plugin observations (Spider's dispatch
861	    state, Sentinel's cost notes).
862	  - `ext` = plugin-keyed metadata-shape data (Reckoner's source,
863	    priority, complexity, rationale, payload, labels; future
864	    provenance / classification / cross-reference data from
865	    other plugins).
866	
867	The metadata-vs-status split mirrors Kubernetes' `metadata` /
868	`status` separation but specialized to our case (plugin-keyed
869	extension rather than K8s's labels + annotations + ownerReferences
870	amalgam).
871	
872	- **Kit-static contribution registries** — `petitioners`
873	  contribution mirrors Clerk's writ-types, Spider's
874	  `rigTemplateMappings`, and the Fabricator's engine designs
875	  (kit declaration, duplicate-collision policy, seal at
876	  `phase:started`). Same shape, same conventions.
877	
878	---
879	
880	## 13. Required Clerk-side schema addition
881	
882	This contract requires one Clerk schema change:
883	
884	```typescript
885	// In WritDoc and CRUD APIs
886	interface WritDoc {
887	  // ... existing fields ...
888	  ext?: Record<string, unknown>;   // plugin-keyed metadata extension
889	}
890	```
891	
892	Plus the symmetric API for plugin-scoped writes:
893	
894	```typescript
895	interface ClerkApi {
896	  // ... existing methods ...
897	
898	  /** Write to a plugin's ext sub-slot (parallel to setWritStatus). */
899	  setWritExt(writId: string, pluginId: string, value: unknown):
900	    Promise<WritDoc>;
901	}
902	```
903	
904	The change is small and benefits the framework broadly, not just
905	this contract — any plugin wanting metadata-shape attachment to
906	writs (provenance, cross-references, classification tags) finds
907	a natural home here. Without it, plugins are forced to abuse the
908	`status` slot for non-status data.
909	
910	The `ext` slot:
911	- Is opt-in per writ (absent by default).
912	- Has identical write-mechanism to `status` (per-plugin sub-slot,
913	  written via dedicated API).
914	- Survives terminal phase transitions (same as `status`).
915	- Is plugin-keyed (no global schema; each plugin owns its
916	  sub-slot).
917	
918	---
919	
920	## 14. Comparison to `petitioner-registration.md`
921	
922	Side-by-side of what this design changes versus the original
923	petitioner-registration framing.
924	
925	### What dissolves
926	
927	- **Petition as a separate concept.** A "petition" is now a writ
928	  in `new` phase carrying `ext['reckoner']`. Same record shape
929	  as any other writ; no parallel entity.
930	- **The petitions book.** Held writs live in Clerk's writs book.
931	  The Reckonings book remains as the evaluation log only.
932	- **`reckoner.emit()` as primary API.** Replaced by
933	  `reckoner.petition()` (helper, Workflow 2) or direct
934	  `clerk.post()` (Workflow 1).
935	- **Closure-bound `PetitionerHandle`.** Replaced by emitter-
936	  stamped `source` field.
937	- **petitionId cross-reference.** Same writ throughout; no two
938	  records to link.
939	- **Petition→writ materialization step.** Approval is a phase
940	  transition, not a record-creation step.
941	- **The `held` lifecycle phase proposal.** Existing `new` phase
942	  already does the holding work.
943	- **Per-source priority allow-lists.** No source-level priority
944	  gating; authority emerges from honest dimension claims.
945	
946	### What stays
947	
948	- **The kit-static petitioner registry.** Same shape as the
949	  original draft (kit declaration, source descriptors,
950	  duplicate-collision policy, seal at `phase:started`) but
951	  smaller — descriptive infrastructure rather than authority
952	  gating. Source field still required on every petition.
953	- **The priority dimension schema.** Same five axes (with
954	  complexity lifted to peer field).
955	- **The Reckoner's scheduling logic.** Out of scope per existing
956	  position; combination function lives in Reckoner-core or
957	  Reckonings-book commission.
958	- **The Reckonings evaluation log.** Reckoner-owned book
959	  separate from writs.
960	- **Lifecycle hooks: none in v0.** Same reasoning.
961	- **Vision-keeper as worked example.** Updated to post held
962	  writs rather than emit petitions, but the substantive use
963	  cases are identical.
964	- **No patron special-case.** Patron-bridge as a normal posting
965	  authority.
966	- **CDC + standing-order feedback channels.** Same three
967	  patterns; keyed off Clerk's writs book instead of a
968	  Reckonings petitions book.
969	
970	### What changes shape
971	
972	- **Clerk schema.** Adds `WritDoc.ext` field and `setWritExt()`
973	  API (smaller than the previously-considered `WritDoc.petitionId`
974	  first-class field; benefits the framework broadly).
975	- **Petitioner code surface.** Posts a writ instead of calling
976	  `reckoner.emit()`. Either through `clerk.post()` directly
977	  (Workflow 1) or through `reckoner.petition()` helper
978	  (Workflow 2). Workflow 3 (post-then-petition) is explicitly
979	  not supported.
980	- **Withdraw mechanics.** Standard `clerk.transition()` (or
981	  `reckoner.withdraw()` helper).
982	- **Configuration.** New `reckoner` key in `guild.json` with
983	  `enforceRegistration` and `disabledSources` (was implicit /
984	  absent in the original draft).
985	
986	### Summary
987	
988	The original draft introduced a parallel "petition" entity and an
989	authority-gate registry. This draft removes the parallel entity
990	(petitions are writs) and demotes the registry to descriptive
991	infrastructure (sources still registered, but trust comes from
992	honest dimension claims, not registry tiers).
993	
994	The contract becomes substantially smaller (~720 lines vs ~850
995	lines) and the framework concept count decreases by one (no
996	"petition" as a distinct entity). The petitioner registry is
997	preserved for its operational value (per-source ops controls,
998	discoverability, metrics).
999	
1000	---
1001	
1002	## 15. Open Questions
1003	
1004	### a. Reckonings book schema
1005	
1006	- **Question.** What are the specific record fields and CDC
1007	  payload shapes of the Reckonings book?
1008	- **Trade-off.** Owned by the parallel Reckonings-book commission
1009	  (`c-modeou1t`).
1010	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When that commission lands, this
1011	  contract's §8 needs a follow-up cross-reference pass.
1012	
1013	### b. Clerk schema change for `ext`
1014	
1015	- **Question.** Detailed shape of `WritDoc.ext` — exact API for
1016	  `setWritExt`, mutability rules, validation, presentation
1017	  projection.
1018	- **Trade-off.** The Clerk-side schema change set owns these
1019	  decisions. This contract commits to "plugin-keyed metadata-
1020	  shape extension slot exists."
1021	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** When the Clerk-side change set
1022	  lands.
1023	
1024	### c. Combination-function design
1025	
1026	- **Question.** How does the Reckoner combine the five priority
1027	  dimensions plus complexity into a single scheduling weight?
1028	- **Trade-off.** Out of scope per §3. Belongs to the Reckoner-
1029	  core or Reckonings-book commission.
1030	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Reckoner-core scheduling prototype.
1031	
1032	### d. Calibration loop for `complexity` claims
1033	
1034	- **Question.** How do petitioner `complexity` claims get
1035	  calibrated against actual token spend? Tracked at
1036	  `c-mohd0luw`.
1037	- **Trade-off.** Operational concern outside this contract.
1038	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Calibration data accumulating to
1039	  the point where a structural mechanism (calibrated by-
1040	  petitioner bias offsets, etc.) would help.
1041	
1042	### e. Lifecycle hook surface
1043	
1044	- **Question.** Future `canRetry` / `onDefer` / `onAccept`
1045	  hooks?
1046	- **Trade-off.** v0 declares no hooks. CDC + standing-order
1047	  observation covers the need.
1048	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** A real `canRetry` use case.
1049	
1050	### f. Patron-bridge apparatus
1051	
1052	- **Question.** What does the patron-bridge apparatus look like?
1053	  CLI surface, MCP tool, commission-post interaction.
1054	- **Trade-off.** Out of contract — separate downstream
1055	  commission. From the Reckoner's perspective, the patron-bridge
1056	  is just another posting authority registered like any other.
1057	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Patron-bridge commission dispatch.
1058	
1059	### g. Standing-order filter capabilities
1060	
1061	- **Question.** §9's standing-order recipes assume filter
1062	  capabilities (e.g. `filterExtSource`, `filterPhase`) that may
1063	  or may not exist in Clockworks today.
1064	- **Trade-off.** This contract relies on Clockworks's standing-
1065	  order substrate without committing to specific filter syntax.
1066	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Implementation pass cross-references
1067	  the actual Clockworks filter API.
1068	
1069	### h. Required-rationale threshold
1070	
1071	- **Question.** Should rationale be required when severity rises
1072	  above a threshold (e.g. `severity: 'critical'` or
1073	  `visionRelation: 'vision-blocker'` requires non-empty
1074	  rationale)? Or remain fully optional?
1075	- **Trade-off.** Optional rationale is honest (don't force a
1076	  guess); required rationale at high tiers raises the bar for
1077	  high-severity claims and aids review.
1078	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** First evidence of high-severity
1079	  claims slipping through without justification, or first patron
1080	  ask for "show me the why" on top-N petitions.
1081	
1082	### i. Per-source throttling / conditional disabling
1083	
1084	- **Question.** Should `disabledSources` grow into a richer per-
1085	  source policy (rate limits, time-windowed disabling,
1086	  conditional-on-X disabling)?
1087	- **Trade-off.** v0 is a simple deny list. Richer policy adds
1088	  configuration surface that's only worth it if real ops needs
1089	  surface.
1090	- **Re-evaluation trigger.** Operator request for finer-grained
1091	  controls.
1092

file.content:
# Reckoner Contract — Held Writs and Dimension-Driven Scheduling

Status: **Draft** (alternative to `petitioner-registration.md`)

> **⚠️ v0 scope.** This document fixes the contract between the
> (forthcoming) petition-scheduler Reckoner and the rest of the
> framework. v0 covers: the petition data shape (priority
> dimensions, complexity, rationale, payload, labels), the
> kit-static petitioner registry, the convention for opting a writ
> into Reckoner gating (`writ.ext['reckoner']`), the Reckoner's
> behavioral contract, the optional `reckoner.petition()` helper,
> the `enforceRegistration` config flag, the Reckonings evaluation
> log, and the trust model. The Reckoner's combination function
> (how dimensions become scheduling weight), the Reckonings book
> schema beyond the evaluation-log shape, the patron-emit surface
> (CLI / MCP), and any non-`vision-keeper` petitioner are
> explicitly out of scope.

> **Reframing note.** This draft is an alternative shape for the
> petitioner-registration contract. It reframes the problem:
> instead of a separate "petition" entity that materializes into a
> writ on approval, a petition IS a writ — in `new` phase, carrying
> Reckoner ext. The Reckoner is one authority among several that
> can transition writs from `new` to an active phase. Side-by-side
> comparison to the original framing is in §13.

---

## Dependencies

```
contract spans: ['clerk', 'spider', 'stacks', 'clockworks']
```

- **The [Clerk](apparatus/clerk.md)** — the writ tracker. Held
  writs in `new` phase are normal Clerk writs; the Reckoner uses
  Clerk's transition API to approve or decline them. Clerk knows
  nothing about the Reckoner; the dependency is one-way.
- **The [Spider](apparatus/spider.md)** — the dispatcher. Spider
  doesn't dispatch writs in `new` phase. Once the Reckoner
  transitions a writ to `open` (or the type-equivalent active
  state), Spider picks it up and dispatches via the writ type's
  rig template.
- **[Stacks](apparatus/stacks.md)** — the storage substrate. The
  Reckoner watches CDC events on the writs book to find writs
  needing consideration.
- **[Clockworks](clockworks.md)** — the event substrate. The auto-
  wired `book.clerk.writs.{created,updated}` events feed the
  Reckoner's CDC handler.

---

## 1. The mechanism

### Posting a Reckoner-gated writ (Workflow 1 — direct)

A petitioner posts a writ in the standard way, with one addition:
the writ carries `writ.ext['reckoner']` with at minimum `source`
and `priority`. That ext is the signal to the Reckoner that this
writ requires its consideration.

```typescript
await clerk.post({
  type:     'mandate',
  title:    'Address vision drift detected at 04:00 UTC',
  body:     '...',
  codex:    'nexus',
  parentId: 'w-...',
  ext: {
    reckoner: {
      source:    'vision-keeper.snapshot',
      priority: {
        visionRelation: 'vision-violator',
        severity:       'serious',
        scope:          'major-area',
        time:           { decay: true, deadline: null },
        domain:         ['quality'],
      },
      complexity: 'bounded',
      rationale:  'Drift detected on snapshot equality check; '
                + 'serious because seal step has dropped commits '
                + '5+ times daily this week.',
      payload:    { /* opaque petitioner-defined data */ },
    },
  },
});
```

The writ exists in `new` phase (Clerk's default for new writs).
Spider doesn't dispatch from `new`. The Reckoner observes CDC,
sees `writ.ext['reckoner']`, validates the source against its
registry (see §5), and adopts responsibility for transitioning the
writ.

This workflow is always available — any apparatus that can call
`clerk.post()` (which is to say, any loaded apparatus) can post a
Reckoner-gated writ this way. No runtime dependency on the
Reckoner API.

### `reckoner.petition()` helper (Workflow 2 — canonical)

For petitioner ergonomics, the Reckoner exposes a thin wrapper
that constructs the writ in the correct shape:

```typescript
interface ReckonerApi {
  /**
   * Post a writ in `new` phase with Reckoner ext set correctly.
   * Validates dimension values against the schema, applies
   * defaults for omitted dimensions, validates source against
   * the registry, and calls clerk.post() under the hood.
   *
   * Equivalent in effect to calling clerk.post() with manual ext
   * construction (Workflow 1); this helper exists for type
   * checking, default handling, and discoverability.
   */
  petition(request: PetitionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Withdraw a held writ by transitioning it to cancelled.
   * Convenience wrapper around clerk.transition(); identical
   * effect to calling Clerk directly.
   */
  withdraw(writId: string, reason?: string): Promise<WritDoc>;
}

interface PetitionRequest {
  // writ fields (passed through to clerk.post)
  type:      string;
  title:     string;
  body:      string;
  codex:     string;
  parentId?: string;

  // ext.reckoner fields
  source:      string;                          // required, registered
  priority:    Priority;                        // see §3
  complexity?: ComplexityTier;                  // see §4
  rationale?:  string;                          // see §2
  payload?:    unknown;                         // opaque
  labels?:     Record<string, string>;          // additive metadata
}
```

The helper is the **canonical** path for ergonomic petitioner
code: a single call, type-checked dimensions, defaults applied,
registry validated at API-call time. Petitioners that prefer
direct posting (Workflow 1) get identical Reckoner behavior; the
helper is a convenience layer, not a gate.

There is **no Workflow 3** (post-then-petition). Adding ext to an
already-posted writ via a separate `reckoner.petition(writId, ...)`
call introduces atomicity concerns (orphaned writs in `new` with
no ext) and serves no current use case. If retroactive gating
becomes needed later, adding a `petition(writId, ...)` overload is
purely additive.

### Reckoner behavior

The Reckoner subscribes to the auto-wired `book.clerk.writs`
events. For each writ event, it inspects the writ:

1. If the writ is **not** in `new` phase, ignore.
2. If the writ does **not** have `ext['reckoner']`, ignore — this
   is not a Reckoner-gated writ; some other authority owns the
   transition.
3. **Source check.** If the writ's `ext['reckoner'].source` is
   **not** registered (see §5):
   - When `enforceRegistration` is true (default): decline the
     writ, transitioning `new` → `cancelled` with a reason
     naming the unknown source.
   - When `enforceRegistration` is false: log a warning and
     proceed.
4. **Per-source ops controls.** If the source is registered but
   currently disabled by Reckoner config, decline the writ with a
   reason naming the disable reason.
5. Otherwise the writ is a held petition. The Reckoner evaluates
   its priority dimensions and complexity against current state
   and decides:
   - **Approve** → transition `new` → `open` (or the type's
     equivalent active state). Spider then picks up.
   - **Defer** → leave in `new`. Append a reckoning entry to the
     Reckonings book noting the deferral. Re-evaluate on the
     next scheduling tick or the next state-relevant event.
   - **Decline** → transition `new` → `cancelled`. The decline
     reason is stamped on the writ via the resolution field.

The Reckoner is one authority among several that can transition
writs out of `new`. Patron manual transitions, planner-driven
automatic transitions, and any other authorities continue to
operate on writs without Reckoner ext.

### Withdrawal

The petitioner withdraws a held writ by transitioning it to
`cancelled` via Clerk's standard transition API:

```typescript
await clerk.transition(writId, 'cancelled', {
  reason: 'Snapshot superseded by drift detected before this ran.',
});
```

The Reckoner's `withdraw(writId, reason?)` helper is a thin
wrapper around the same call.

If withdrawal lands while the writ is still in `new` (the common
case), the Reckoner's CDC handler stops considering it. If
withdrawal lands after approval (writ already in `open` or
further), it follows the normal lifecycle for cancelling
in-flight work — same as any other writ being cancelled mid-
execution. No special Reckoner logic required.

### After approval

Once approved, the writ is a normal active writ. Spider
dispatches; the appropriate rig runs; engines do the work;
lifecycle proceeds normally. The Reckoner's involvement ends at
approval.

The data the petitioner attached in `writ.ext['reckoner'].payload`
is available to the running engine via standard writ access — the
engine reads it if it cares. Most rigs won't.

For animas working the writ, no special tools are needed —
everything they need is on the writ. If structured payload
inspection becomes common, the Reckoner can ship a small read-
tool that pretty-prints `writ.ext['reckoner']`, but it's not
required for correctness.

---

## 2. The petition shape

The full `writ.ext['reckoner']` shape:

```typescript
interface ReckonerExt {
  /**
   * Required. Identifies the petitioner. Must match a registered
   * petitioner descriptor (see §5) when enforceRegistration is on.
   */
  source: string;

  /**
   * Required. Multi-dimensional priority. See §3.
   */
  priority: Priority;

  /**
   * Optional petitioner-side coarse cost estimate. Refined by the
   * Astrolabe at plan time. See §4.
   */
  complexity?: ComplexityTier;

  /**
   * Optional free-form justification for the priority claim.
   * Distinct from writ.body (which describes the work).
   * Documents why the dimensions are claimed at the levels stated.
   * Useful for code review, audit, and observability of
   * mis-claims.
   */
  rationale?: string;

  /**
   * Opaque petitioner-defined structured data. The Reckoner
   * stores it but does not introspect; the rig's implementation
   * engine reads it if needed.
   */
  payload?: unknown;

  /**
   * Additive non-priority metadata. Multi-instance discrimination
   * (e.g. `'vision-keeper.io/vision-id': 'nexus'`),
   * observability hints, diagnostic tags. See §11.
   */
  labels?: Record<string, string>;
}
```

The split between `priority` (how much does this matter?),
`complexity` (what does it cost?), and `rationale` (why is this
claim warranted?) is intentional — these are conceptually
