content.content:
1	# The Reckonings Book — Schema & CDC Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft**
4	
5	Owner plugin: `reckoner` · Book name: `reckonings` · Sibling docs:
6	[clockworks.md](clockworks.md), [apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md),
7	[apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md),
8	[apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md),
9	[apparatus/sentinel.md](apparatus/sentinel.md).
10	
11	> **⚠️ Forward-looking design.** This document specifies the
12	> Reckoner's evaluation-log book before the Reckoner core itself is
13	> commissioned. The plugin id `reckoner` and the existing
14	> `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` (formerly `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus`;
15	> a narrow queue-observer that emits Lattice pulses for `writ-stuck` /
16	> `writ-failed` / `queue-drained`) have been renamed in a separate
17	> commission so this id can be reused for the new petition-scheduling
18	> Reckoner. Nothing in `packages/plugins/sentinel/` is touched by this
19	> design — see Open Questions for the integration.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Intent
24	
25	The Reckonings book is the Reckoner's **evaluation journal**: one
26	append-only row per meaningful consideration, persisted to The Stacks
27	as `stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')`. The journal is
28	**event-shaped, not tick-shaped** — the Reckoner writes a row when a
29	consideration produced a state transition (accept / defer / decline)
30	or when a re-evaluation was substantive enough to record (e.g. a
31	wake-up signal fired and the Reckoner re-weighed against current
32	conditions). Ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition, or
33	re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
34	produce no row.
35	
36	This means **absence-of-row is itself a signal**: a patron, a
37	downstream petitioner, or a future ethnographer asking "what has the
38	Reckoner decided about this petition?" gets the answer from the rows
39	that exist; the lack of any row for a petition since timestamp T means
40	"the Reckoner has not reached it yet, or no condition has changed
41	that warranted re-evaluation."
42	
43	*Which* petitions get weighed on a given tick — and the per-tick
44	budget that bounds how far down the priority order the Reckoner walks
45	— is a Reckoner-core scoping-policy decision, not a journal-schema
46	decision, and lives in the Reckoner-core commission. The schema here
47	accommodates either a sweep-everything-every-tick policy or a
48	priority-bounded walk; the journal records what was considered, not
49	the policy that picked it.
50	
51	This doc settles the schema, the index set, the retention stance, the
52	CDC attachment, and the conceptual framing relative to the
53	petition-state book that lives next door. It is decision-supporting
54	prose only — downstream Reckoner-core implementers consume this design
55	as a settled contract; no code, schema declaration, plugin manifest, or
56	`guild.json` block ships from this commission.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Book Identity
61	
62	`stacks.book('reckoner', 'reckonings')` — `ownerId` is `reckoner`, the
63	plugin id of the new Reckoner-core; book name is the plural noun
64	`reckonings`, matching the sibling event-log naming convention
65	(`events`, `pulses`, `sessions`, `transcripts`).
66	
67	The auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks event surface for this book is
68	therefore `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
69	`book.reckoner.reckonings.updated`, and
70	`book.reckoner.reckonings.deleted`. Of these only the `.created` event
71	ever fires in practice — see the Immutability section below — and that
72	single event is the channel petitioners subscribe to (see CDC
73	Attachment).
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Record Schema
78	
79	### Outcome enum
80	
81	Every Reckonings record carries a four-state `outcome`:
82	
83	```
84	'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op'
85	```
86	
87	- **accepted** — the Reckoner created a writ for the petition and
88	  flipped the petition state to `accepted`. The resulting writ flows
89	  into the standard rig pipeline (Distiller → Sage → Artificer), same
90	  as a patron-posted commission.
91	- **deferred** — the Reckoner held the petition until a wake-up signal
92	  fires, with structured reason metadata (see below).
93	- **declined** — the Reckoner rejected the petition on validity
94	  grounds. The decline scope is intentionally narrow (see Decline
95	  Reasons) — "this work isn't worth doing on merit" is patron
96	  territory and produces a `withdrawn` transition by the patron, not
97	  a Reckoner-issued decline.
98	- **no-op** — the Reckoner re-weighed the petition this tick against
99	  changed conditions and chose to hold without a state transition.
100	  Typical examples: a deferred petition's wake-up signal fired and the
101	  Reckoner re-evaluated but still held; a sibling resolution shifted
102	  the priority queue and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's
103	  position; capacity changed and the Reckoner reconsidered. A `no-op`
104	  row records that the Reckoner did real work — not the heartbeat of
105	  the polling loop. See No-op Handling for the absence-of-row
106	  convention that distinguishes "we weighed and held" from "we haven't
107	  reached this yet."
108	
109	`withdrawn` is a petitioner-initiated transition and is **not** a
110	Reckoner-consideration outcome, so it does not produce a Reckonings
111	row.
112	
113	### Petition shape (settled upstream)
114	
115	The Reckoner reads petitions from the petition-state book; the schema
116	of that book is settled by an adjacent commission and reproduced here
117	so this doc is self-contained. Each petition carries:
118	
119	- `source` — petitioner identity (vision-keeper, future tech-debt
120	  watchers, the laboratory introspection writer, ad-hoc patron
121	  petitions, …).
122	- `intent` — short imperative sentence describing the work the
123	  petitioner wants done.
124	- `rationale` — long-form prose justifying the petition.
125	- `priority_signals` — a structured block: `urgency` (an enum,
126	  `immediate | urgent | normal | low`) plus an optional
127	  `strategic_ref` pointing into a vision-document or roadmap entry.
128	- `context_anchors` — file paths, writ ids, transcript ids, and
129	  similar handles a downstream specifier can use as starting points.
130	
131	There is no pre-built brief or spec on a petition. Specification work
132	happens **after** acceptance through the existing Distiller → Sage
133	pipeline, the same path patron-posted commissions take.
134	
135	### Petition lifecycle (settled upstream)
136	
137	```
138	new → pending → { accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn }
139	```
140	
141	- `accepted`, `declined`, and `withdrawn` are terminal.
142	- `deferred → pending` re-awakens on a wake-up signal.
143	- `withdrawn` is petitioner-initiated and produces **no** Reckonings
144	  row — the Reckoner never weighed it as part of its own
145	  consideration loop.
146	
147	### Record body
148	
149	```typescript
150	// Illustrative — declared in the Reckoner-core commission, not here.
151	interface ReckoningDoc {
152	  /** Unique id (`rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`). Sortable by creation time. */
153	  id: string;
154	
155	  /** The petition this record is about. */
156	  petitionId: string;
157	
158	  /** Lean denormalized projection — see "Lean snapshot" below. */
159	  source: string;
160	  urgency: 'immediate' | 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';
161	
162	  /** Outcome enum — drives the discriminated-union reason fields. */
163	  outcome: 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';
164	
165	  /**
166	   * Triggering Clockworks event id — the `clockworks.timer` row that
167	   * produced this tick. Optional only on the patron fast path.
168	   * See "Tick identity".
169	   */
170	  tickEventId?: string;
171	
172	  /** ISO timestamp when the Reckoner completed this consideration. */
173	  consideredAt: string;
174	
175	  // ── Outcome-keyed reason metadata (flat optionals) ──────────────
176	  // Consumer types should encode the iff-outcome invariant via a
177	  // discriminated union; the persisted shape stays flat so indexes
178	  // can name top-level fields directly.
179	
180	  /** outcome === 'declined' */
181	  declineReason?:
182	    | 'malformed'
183	    | 'duplicate'
184	    | 'policy_violation'
185	    | 'source_banned'
186	    | 'other';
187	  remediationHint?: string;
188	
189	  /** outcome === 'deferred' */
190	  deferReason?:
191	    | 'priority'
192	    | 'queue_depth'
193	    | 'time_hold'
194	    | 'patron_policy'
195	    | 'other';
196	  deferUntil?: string;            // ISO timestamp, optional
197	  deferSignal?: string;           // event-pattern reservation, optional
198	  deferCount?: number;            // running count of times deferred
199	  firstDeferredAt?: string;       // ISO timestamp
200	  lastDeferredAt?: string;        // ISO timestamp
201	  deferNote?: string;             // freeform short note
202	
203	  /** outcome === 'accepted' */
204	  writId?: string;                // the writ created on acceptance
205	  acceptedAt?: string;            // ISO timestamp
206	}
207	```
208	
209	### Identity (`id`)
210	
211	Record ids use the Stacks `generateId('rk')` convention, producing the
212	form `rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`. The two-letter prefix is a deliberate
213	choice:
214	
215	- Single-letter prefixes (`p-` for pulses, `w-` for writs, `e-` for
216	  events, `d-` for dispatches, `c-` for commissions) are taken.
217	- The next plausible single letter (`r-`) is one keystroke away from
218	  Spider's `rig-` prefix and would invite confusion in CLI surfaces
219	  where ids are pasted side-by-side.
220	- `rk-` keeps the prefix short while being unambiguous against
221	  `rig-`. The same logic applies to any future `rc-` (Reckoner
222	  control plane) artifact — every Reckoner-domain id starts with `r`,
223	  the second letter discriminates.
224	
225	The base36-timestamp prefix sorts consistently with `createdAt` for
226	same-millisecond emissions, so list views ordered by `createdAt desc,
227	id desc` produce stable output across repeated calls — same precedent
228	the Lattice uses for pulse-id tie-breaking.
229	
230	### Lean snapshot vs. fat denormalization
231	
232	The record carries `petitionId` plus a small, deliberate projection of
233	two petition fields — `source` and `priority_signals.urgency` — at the
234	top level. It does **not** embed the full petition body and does
235	**not** carry a foreign-key-only reference to the petition row.
236	
237	The two projected fields are the ones that drive hot filter queries:
238	
239	- "show me everything the Reckoner did with petitions from
240	  `vision-keeper`" — `source` filter.
241	- "show me every consideration of an `urgent` petition since T" —
242	  `urgency` and `consideredAt` filter.
243	
244	Embedding only these two avoids a join on every per-petition timeline
245	or per-source audit query, while keeping the record byte-budget close
246	to the lattice/pulses precedent. The full petition row remains the
247	source of truth; the Reckonings record is a snapshot at the moment of
248	consideration.
249	
250	The alternative (a fat record carrying `intent`, `rationale`,
251	`context_anchors`, etc.) was considered and rejected: those fields are
252	not filtered against, they grow with the petition's free-form prose,
253	and they would inflate every row of an append-forever book. The
254	animator's lean-record / heavy-blob split (sessions + transcripts) is
255	the precedent — but Reckonings stays as a single book because the
256	record body is already lean enough that a heavy-blob sibling would be
257	empty.
258	
259	### Tick identity
260	
261	The Reckoner's v0 trigger is a fixed-interval polling tick implemented
262	as a Clockworks `schedule:` standing order. Every fire writes a
263	synthesized `clockworks.timer` event row into `clockworks/events` (see
264	[clockworks.md → Scheduled Standing Orders](clockworks.md#scheduled-standing-orders))
265	with a unique event id of the form `e-<base36_ts>-<hex>`.
266	
267	Each Reckonings record stamps the triggering `clockworks.timer` event id
268	into a `tickEventId` field. Together with `consideredAt`, this gives
269	the consumer two complementary handles:
270	
271	- **`tickEventId`** — exact-match join to the dispatch row, the
272	  schedule entry, and every sibling Reckonings row produced by the
273	  same tick.
274	- **`consideredAt`** — time-range filter for since-T sweeps and
275	  per-petition timeline ordering without going through the events
276	  book.
277	
278	The doc deliberately reuses the framework-emitted `clockworks.timer` id
279	rather than synthesizing a new "Reckoner tick id" — it earns no second
280	piece of identity. There is one exception:
281	
282	#### Patron fast-path: `tickEventId` is optional
283	
284	The settled patron fast-path bypasses the Reckoner tick entirely. A
285	patron-posted petition with `urgency: 'immediate'` is auto-accepted at
286	post time, with the writ created synchronously and no waiting for the
287	next tick. The Reckonings row for an `immediate` acceptance is still
288	written so the audit trail is uniform — but because the row is written
289	**outside** any tick, there is no `clockworks.timer` event id to stamp.
290	`tickEventId` is therefore optional, not required: present on every
291	non-fast-path row, absent on patron-fast-path acceptances.
292	
293	### Outcome-keyed reason metadata layout
294	
295	All reason fields live at the top level of the record (flat
296	optionals), with an iff-outcome invariant the consumer types encode as
297	a discriminated union:
298	
299	| Outcome     | Top-level fields populated                                                                                  |
300	|-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
301	| `accepted`  | `writId`, `acceptedAt`                                                                                       |
302	| `deferred`  | `deferReason`, `deferUntil?`, `deferSignal?`, `deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, `lastDeferredAt`, `deferNote?` |
303	| `declined`  | `declineReason`, `remediationHint?`                                                                          |
304	| `no-op`     | (none — only the projection and the tick stamp)                                                              |
305	
306	The flat layout is required so the index set (see Indexes) can name
307	top-level fields directly — `declineReason` for the "decline-by-reason
308	audit" query is the pivotal example. A nested `reason: { … }` blob
309	would force every query against reason metadata to filter in-process
310	on JSON-extracted values, defeating the index-driven design.
311	
312	This matches the lattice/pulses convention of putting filterable
313	fields at the top level and reserving free-form context for a
314	purpose-built blob; the Reckonings record has no free-form blob
315	because every meaningful field is named and filterable.
316	
317	#### Writer-enforced invariant
318	
319	The iff-outcome invariant — "a row populates exactly the reason fields
320	keyed to its `outcome` and no others" — is **writer-enforced by the
321	Reckoner core**. The Stacks book schema does not validate it: persisted
322	records can in principle hold inconsistent shapes (e.g. an `accepted`
323	row carrying a stray `declineReason`). The contract is that the
324	Reckoner core is the only writer of this book, and it constructs every
325	row through a single discriminated-union builder before persisting.
326	Consumer types decode against the same discriminated union, so a
327	malformed row would surface as a parse error at read time — not as a
328	silently-tolerated invalid state.
329	
330	### Decline reasons (settled upstream)
331	
332	The decline-reason enum is intentionally narrow:
333	
334	```
335	'malformed'        // petition shape is invalid (missing source, etc.)
336	'duplicate'        // a prior petition with the same intent is open
337	'policy_violation' // petition violates a guild-declared policy
338	'source_banned'    // the source has been blacklisted
339	'other'            // freeform — `remediationHint` carries the detail
340	```
341	
342	The narrowness is the point: the Reckoner's decline scope is "petition
343	validity," not "merit." A petition that is well-formed but unwise
344	gets a `withdrawn` transition from the patron — the Reckoner never
345	issues a merit-based decline.
346	
347	### Defer reasons (settled upstream)
348	
349	```
350	'priority'       // a higher-priority petition is ahead in the queue
351	'queue_depth'    // the active rig set is full; hold for capacity
352	'time_hold'      // operator-set hold until a wall-clock time
353	'patron_policy'  // guild-policy says this category is paused
354	'other'          // freeform — `deferNote` carries the detail
355	```
356	
357	`deferUntil` is populated when the Reckoner can name a wall-clock
358	time at which to revisit (a `time_hold` or a delayed-priority
359	re-evaluation). `deferSignal` is populated when the Reckoner reserves
360	an event pattern as a wake-up trigger (e.g. "re-weigh when
361	`book.reckoner.petitions.updated` fires for this petition"). At least
362	one of `deferUntil` and `deferSignal` is populated on a deferred row
363	in normal operation; both being empty is allowed for the rare
364	`other`-reason hold but should produce a `deferNote` for the audit
365	trail.
366	
367	The actual wake-up dispatch path — how the Reckoner converts a
368	populated `deferSignal` into a Clockworks standing-order subscription,
369	how `deferUntil` is converted into a `schedule:` order, and how the
370	re-tick fires against the deferred petition — is owned by the
371	Reckoner-core commission. This doc names the schema fields the
372	mechanism stamps; the mechanism itself is settled there.
373	
374	`deferCount`, `firstDeferredAt`, and `lastDeferredAt` are running
375	counters: each new deferral on the same petition increments
376	`deferCount` and refreshes `lastDeferredAt`, while
377	`firstDeferredAt` is preserved across deferrals as the petition's
378	first-seen-as-deferred timestamp. The Reckoner reads the prior
379	Reckonings row for the petition to compute the running counter; the
380	journal is its own source of truth for the deferral history.
381	
382	**The counter advances only on `outcome: 'deferred'` rows.** A
383	`no-op` row produced when the Reckoner re-weighed a deferred petition
384	and chose to keep holding does **not** increment `deferCount`. The
385	counter records distinct deferrals, not re-evaluations of an existing
386	hold; "how many times has the Reckoner deferred this petition?" is
387	answered by `count(*) WHERE petitionId = X AND outcome = 'deferred'`,
388	and the running counter on the most recent deferred row matches that
389	count.
390	
391	### Acceptance metadata
392	
393	A row with `outcome: 'accepted'` carries:
394	
395	- `writId` — the Clerk writ id the Reckoner created on acceptance.
396	  This is a forward link into the rig pipeline; the writ flows
397	  through Distiller → Sage → Artificer the same as any patron-posted
398	  commission.
399	- `acceptedAt` — ISO timestamp of the acceptance, distinct from
400	  `consideredAt` only in the patron-fast-path case where the
401	  acceptance happens outside a tick. In the normal case the two are
402	  identical.
403	
404	---
405	
406	## No-op Handling
407	
408	A `no-op` row records that **the Reckoner did real evaluative work
409	that did not produce a state transition**. It is not the heartbeat of
410	the polling loop — ticks where the Reckoner did not reach a petition,
411	or re-encountered an unchanged condition and held the same conclusion,
412	produce no row at all.
413	
414	### When a no-op row is written
415	
416	The Reckoner writes a `no-op` row when a consideration was
417	*substantive* but the conclusion was still "hold." The canonical
418	cases:
419	
420	- A deferred petition's `deferSignal` fired (its wake-up event
421	  pattern matched). The Reckoner re-weighed against current state
422	  and chose to keep holding rather than transition. The wake-up was
423	  meaningful even though the outcome did not change.
424	- A deferred petition's `deferUntil` deadline passed and the
425	  Reckoner's scheduled re-tick re-evaluated it. Same logic — the
426	  re-evaluation was real work, recorded, even if the conclusion did
427	  not transition.
428	- A pending petition was re-weighed because a sibling resolved
429	  (acceptance or withdrawal shifted the priority queue) or capacity
430	  changed, and the Reckoner re-checked this petition's standing.
431	  The re-evaluation was triggered by a change in conditions, not by
432	  the tick itself.
433	
434	### When a row is **not** written
435	
436	- The Reckoner did not reach the petition this tick (priority-bounded
437	  walk stopped before it). No row.
438	- The Reckoner ticked, encountered the petition, but no condition had
439	  changed since the last consideration — same priority order, same
440	  capacity, same wake-up state. No row. The prior conclusion stands
441	  by inheritance.
442	
443	### Absence-of-row is the signal
444	
445	The "did anyone look at me?" question is answered by **any** row
446	existing for the petition. A petition with no rows has not yet been
447	reached or has had no condition change warrant re-evaluation; that is
448	itself meaningful information. A petition with a recent terminal row
449	(`accepted` / `declined`) has its outcome on file. A petition with a
450	deferred row and several no-op rows has been actively re-weighed
451	multiple times since the last transition.
452	
453	This convention shifts a small amount of reasoning onto callers ("no
454	row" means a specific thing) in exchange for an event-shaped journal
455	that scales with decision events rather than with tick frequency. The
456	storage-growth math (next section) reflects this design.
457	
458	### No-op records carry the same projection as state-transition records
459	
460	When a `no-op` row *is* written, it carries `source` and `urgency`
461	exactly like every other outcome — same lean snapshot, same tick
462	stamp, same `consideredAt`. The modest byte savings of a stripped
463	no-op shape don't justify branching the read path for every consumer
464	query: filters like "since T, all considerations of urgent petitions"
465	must work uniformly across all four outcomes, and the indexes that
466	support those filters need every row to be the same shape.
467	
468	This uniformity also keeps the discriminated-union consumer type
469	clean: no fork between "full record" and "stub record"; the
470	discriminant is `outcome`, and the optional reason fields default to
471	absent.
472	
473	---
474	
475	## Retention
476	
477	**Append-only forever.** The Reckonings book has no rolling-window
478	default, no built-in archival relay, no prune job. Every row the
479	Reckoner ever writes persists until an explicit operator action
480	removes it.
481	
482	This matches the sibling event-log books — `clockworks/events`,
483	`clockworks/event_dispatches`, `lattice/pulses`,
484	`animator/sessions`, `animator/transcripts` all default to unbounded
485	growth. The lattice-apparatus and animator-apparatus docs both name
486	retention as a future commission with no current trigger; the
487	Reckonings book inherits the same stance.
488	
489	A rolling-window default would silently lose audit history. The
490	journal's load-bearing job is "show me everything that ever happened
491	to this petition" — a 90-day window quietly drops the row a
492	post-mortem six months later needs.
493	
494	### Storage-growth math
495	
496	The journal is event-shaped, so volume scales with decision events
497	rather than with tick frequency. A representative steady-state model
498	based on the v0 cadence (1 tick / minute) and a moderately active
499	guild:
500	
501	```
502	state-transition rows (accepts + defers + declines)
503	  ≈ 2–10 / hour during active operation
504	  ≈ 0–2 / hour during idle stretches
505	
506	substantive no-op rows (wake-up re-evaluations, sibling resolutions
507	                        prompting re-weighs, capacity changes)
508	  ≈ 5–30 / hour during active operation
509	  ≈ 0–5 / hour during idle stretches
510	
511	steady-state composite
512	  ≈ 1–3 rows / minute averaged across active and idle periods
513	  ≈ 525,000 – 1,575,000 rows / year
514	  ≈ 250 MB – 800 MB / year   at  ~500 bytes / row (lean projection)
515	```
516	
517	This is roughly an order of magnitude below what a sweep-everything-
518	every-tick policy would produce (the earlier draft of this doc
519	projected ~5.3M rows / year on that assumption). The order-of-
520	magnitude difference is the load-bearing payoff of the event-shaped
521	design: the journal records decisions, not heartbeats.
522	
523	SQLite handles tables at this scale comfortably with the indexes
524	declared below — primary-key lookups stay sub-millisecond, the
525	indexed range scans on `consideredAt` and the per-petition timeline
526	use the compound indexes directly, and table size at this magnitude
527	is well within the Animator's transcripts-book scale (~30–300 MB /
528	day) that the same substrate is already exercised at.
529	
530	Trip-wires that warrant revisiting:
531	
532	- An order-of-magnitude increase in tick cadence (e.g. 10×
533	  faster) **combined with** a Reckoner-core scoping policy that
534	  produces no-ops more eagerly than the substantive-only convention
535	  above.
536	- A sustained 6-month measurement that exceeds 2M rows / year
537	  (more than 25% above the projection's upper bound).
538	- An operator-visible query latency regression on the per-petition
539	  timeline or since-T sweeps.
540	
541	The trip-wires belong in Open Questions, not in the v0 retention
542	design.
543	
544	### Future archival pattern (named, not built)
545	
546	When an operator does need to bound the book, the natural shape — to
547	be designed by a future commission, not this one — is a separate
548	archival relay subscribed to a wall-clock schedule that:
549	
550	1. Selects rows older than a configured threshold via a compound
551	   index (`['consideredAt']`).
552	2. Writes them to a sibling `reckonings_archive` book, an external
553	   sink (the Sentinel apparatus, the Laboratory's CDC ingestion), or
554	   both.
555	3. Deletes the live rows in the same transaction the archive write
556	   commits in.
557	
558	The doc names this only as an opt-in; building it before the
559	trip-wires fire would be premature.
560	
561	---
562	
563	## Query Patterns and Indexes
564	
565	The index set is filter-shape-driven: every entry traces back to a
566	named query the Reckoner or its consumers run. Speculative indexes
567	are not added.
568	
569	### Named queries
570	
571	| Query | Filter shape | Index used |
572	|-------|--------------|------------|
573	| **Per-petition timeline** — show every consideration of petition X, oldest first | `petitionId = X` ORDER BY `consideredAt asc` | `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']` |
574	| **Since-T sweep** — every Reckonings row produced since timestamp T | `consideredAt >= T` | `consideredAt` |
575	| **Decline-by-reason audit** — every petition declined for reason R | `outcome = 'declined' AND declineReason = R` | `declineReason` (with the `outcome` filter narrowing the candidate set further) |
576	| **Per-source filtering** — every consideration of petitions emitted by source S | `source = S` (optionally + `consideredAt`) | `source` |
577	| **Recent-by-outcome** — most recent N rows for outcome O | `outcome = O` ORDER BY `consideredAt desc` | `['outcome', 'consideredAt']` |
578	| **Urgency-led timeline** — recent considerations of immediate / urgent petitions, regardless of outcome | `urgency = U` ORDER BY `consideredAt desc` | `['urgency', 'consideredAt']` |
579	| **Outcome-only filter** — count or list rows by outcome | `outcome = O` | `outcome` |
580	
581	### Declared index set
582	
583	> **Illustrative — declared in the Reckoner-core commission, not here.**
584	> The list below is the contract this design hands forward; the actual
585	> `indexes:` block ships from the Reckoner-core book registration.
586	
587	```typescript
588	indexes: [
589	  'petitionId',
590	  'consideredAt',
591	  'outcome',
592	  'source',
593	  'urgency',
594	  'declineReason',
595	  ['outcome', 'consideredAt'],
596	  ['urgency', 'consideredAt'],
597	  ['petitionId', 'consideredAt'],
598	]
599	```
600	
601	Tracing each entry back to the queries it supports:
602	
603	- **`petitionId`** — bare-key existence checks ("does any row exist
604	  for this petition?") and the foreign-key style join from the
605	  petition-state book; superseded for ordered timelines by
606	  `['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`.
607	- **`consideredAt`** — the unconditional since-T sweep, used by
608	  recent-history surfaces (Oculus pages, the future Sentinel
609	  apparatus's archival selector, vision-keeper's "what changed since
610	  my last poll" check).
611	- **`outcome`** — outcome-only filters and counts; standalone for
612	  uses that don't need a time bound.
613	- **`source`** — per-source audit (every consideration of petitions
614	  from a single petitioner). The petitioners-registration commission
615	  will likely want to surface this as part of an operator's
616	  per-petitioner page.
617	- **`urgency`** — priority-led queries: "every immediate petition
618	  the Reckoner has ever weighed," "show me the urgent backlog the
619	  Reckoner has touched in the last hour." Priority is the
620	  Reckoner's organizing axis, so an urgency-led query is a primary
621	  dashboard shape, not a secondary filter.
622	- **`declineReason`** — the decline-by-reason audit. The flat
623	  schema layout is what makes this an indexable top-level field; a
624	  nested `reason: { … }` would not.
625	- **`['outcome', 'consideredAt']`** — recent-by-outcome compound,
626	  serving the most common dashboard query: "what did the Reckoner
627	  decide in the last hour, grouped by outcome." The leading
628	  `outcome` column is low-cardinality (4 values), so this index
629	  doubles as a fast histogram input.
630	- **`['urgency', 'consideredAt']`** — urgency-led timeline without
631	  a re-sort. Pairs with the standalone `urgency` index for
632	  priority-leading dashboard widgets and operator queries that
633	  combine "level X petitions" with "since T."
634	- **`['petitionId', 'consideredAt']`** — per-petition timeline
635	  ordering without a re-sort. Critical for the petitioner-side "show
636	  me my petition's full history" view.
637	
638	`tickEventId` is not indexed. The expected access pattern
639	is "look up the Reckonings row by id, then walk to the tick" — not
640	"find every row produced by tick T," which is rare and tolerates the
641	full scan or a join through `consideredAt`. Adding the index later
642	costs one schema migration and earns its keep only if the access
643	pattern changes.
644	
645	---
646	
647	## Immutability
648	
649	Reckonings records are **strictly immutable.** They carry `consideredAt`
650	and no `updatedAt`; once written, the row is never patched.
651	
652	### Why no `updatedAt`
653	
654	The Lattice's `pulses` book carries `updatedAt` because pulses have a
655	mutable field — `deliveryState` (`pending → delivered/failed`) — that
656	the Lattice itself rewrites as dispatches progress. The Reckonings
657	book has no analogous mutable field. There is no producer that would
658	mutate a record in place:
659	
660	- The outcome is final at consideration time.
661	- The reason metadata is derived from the petition state at the
662	  moment of consideration; a later re-evaluation produces a new row,
663	  not a patch on the old one.
664	- The `writId` for an accepted petition does not change.
665	- Defer-counter updates (`deferCount`, `lastDeferredAt`) accumulate
666	  by writing a new deferred row, not by mutating the prior one — the
667	  per-deferral history is the journal's load-bearing output.
668	
669	Adding `updatedAt` with no producer would be structure with no
670	consumer.
671	
672	### Future mutation needs
673	
674	If a future commission needs to attach mutable annotations to a
675	Reckonings row — operator notes, post-hoc tags, classifications from
676	a Sentinel-style auditor — those belong in a sibling overlay book
677	keyed by Reckonings id, not in-place mutation of the journal. The
678	journal is the durable record; overlays are layered on top.
679	
680	---
681	
682	## CDC Attachment
683	
684	### Channel name
685	
686	Petitioner subscribers wire to the auto-wired Stacks → Clockworks
687	event:
688	
689	```
690	book.reckoner.reckonings.created
691	```
692	
693	The change-event payload is the Stacks `ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>`
694	passed through verbatim by the Clockworks CDC bridge (see
695	[clockworks.md → Book change events](clockworks.md#book-change-events-stacks-auto-wiring)
696	and [event-catalog.md → CDC Events](../reference/event-catalog.md#cdc-events)).
697	Subscribers that want to filter to a specific outcome do so in their
698	relay handler, against `event.entry.outcome`:
699	
700	```typescript
701	// Illustrative — vision-keeper's accepted-only relay sketch.
702	relay({
703	  name: 'vision-keeper-on-accept',
704	  handler: async (event) => {
705	    const change = event.payload as ChangeEvent<ReckoningDoc>;
706	    if (change.type !== 'create') return;
707	    if (change.entry.outcome !== 'accepted') return;
708	    // … react to the accepted petition.
709	  },
710	});
711	```
712	
713	The in-relay payload filter is one line. Adding a named-events surface
714	(`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, `reckoning.declined`,
715	`reckoning.no-op`) would double the write path with no second consumer
716	auto-wiring cannot serve. Named events are an explicitly considered
717	and rejected alternative; see Open Questions for the conditions under
718	which they would earn their keep.
719	
720	### No carve-out from auto-wiring
721	
722	The Clockworks apparatus carves `clockworks/events` itself out of CDC
723	auto-wiring because a watcher on the events book would observe its
724	own emit and re-emit forever — see
725	[clockworks.ts: auto-wiring carve-out](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/clockworks.ts).
726	Reckonings has **no** such recursion: the Reckoner does not write a
727	Reckonings row in response to a Reckonings change event, and no
728	downstream petitioner loops back into the Reckoner's tick path
729	through the Reckonings stream.
730	
731	The Reckonings book therefore takes **no carve-out**. The auto-wired
732	`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` event fires normally and is the
733	single CDC channel petitioners subscribe to.
734	
735	If the doc adopted named events instead, the carve-out question would
736	be moot — but then we would be writing every change twice (once via
737	auto-wire, once via the named emit). Keeping the Reckonings book in
738	the auto-wired path is what makes the single-write design work.
739	
740	### Validator namespace observation
741	
742	The `reckoner.` and `reckoning.` prefixes are **not** currently
743	reserved in the Clockworks signal validator
744	(`packages/plugins/clockworks/src/signal-validator.ts` —
745	`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` covers `anima.`, `commission.`, `tool.`,
746	`migration.`, `guild.`, `standing-order.`, `session.`, `schedule.`).
747	This is **not load-bearing in v0**: the auto-wired
748	`book.reckoner.reckonings.created` channel is the only event the
749	Reckonings book produces, and the `book.` prefix is also unreserved
750	(see [event-catalog.md: reserved-namespace gap](../reference/event-catalog.md#reserved-namespaces))
751	so adding `reckoner.` to the reserved list would not close the
752	existing gap on CDC events anyway.
753	
754	If a future commission earns the named-events surface (the
755	alternative rejected here), it must extend the validator's
756	`RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` list at the same time so animas cannot
757	forge `reckoning.accepted` / `reckoning.deferred` / etc. via the
758	`signal` tool. That coupling is owned by whichever future commission
759	introduces named emission — it is **not** done by this design.
760	
761	---
762	
763	## Journal vs. Materialized View
764	
765	The Reckoner ecosystem uses two books with deliberately different
766	grains:
767	
768	| Book                          | Role                | Grain                                        |
769	|-------------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------------|
770	| `reckoner/petitions` (separate commission) | Materialized view   | One row per petition, current state, fast lookup |
771	| `reckoner/reckonings` (this doc) | Event journal       | One row per consideration, full history       |
772	
773	**The two CDC channels are not duplicative.** They differ in grain:
774	
775	- `book.reckoner.petitions.{created,updated,deleted}` fires on
776	  state-transitions of a petition. A subscriber wiring to
777	  `book.reckoner.petitions.updated` and filtering on
778	  `event.entry.state === 'accepted'` sees the **state-of-the-world**
779	  change at the moment of acceptance, but does not see the
780	  Reckoner's repeated weighings of that same petition while it was
781	  pending or deferred.
782	- `book.reckoner.reckonings.created` fires on every consideration —
783	  state-changing or not. A subscriber wiring here sees the
784	  **decision history** of every petition the Reckoner has weighed.
785	
786	Asking the petition-state book to double as a journal would force an
787	O(n) scan of every petition row to reconstruct deferral history, and
788	would fail entirely for no-op considerations that produced no
789	state-transition row to scan.
790	
791	Asking the journal to double as a materialized view would force every
792	"what is the current state of petition X?" query to walk the journal
793	backward looking for the most recent terminal row. The petition row
794	has its own state machine and its own metadata (deferral counters,
795	strategic-ref, withdraw-trigger handles); it is **not** merely
796	derived from the journal.
797	
798	The two books exist together. Petitioners that want "act on every
799	state transition" subscribe to the petitions CDC channel.
800	Petitioners that want "audit every consideration" subscribe to the
801	reckonings CDC channel. Most petitioners only need the first. The
802	journal is the second.
803	
804	---
805	
806	## Precedent Comparison
807	
808	The Reckonings book is the fifth book in the guild's family of
809	event-log substrates. It inherits the established shape from its
810	predecessors and diverges where the load-bearing constraints differ.
811	The four citations:
812	
813	- `clockworks/events` — `EventDoc` in
814	  [packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts)
815	  (`{ id, name, payload, emitter, firedAt, processed }`), index set
816	  `['name', 'processed', 'firedAt', ['processed', 'firedAt']]`.
817	- `clockworks/event_dispatches` — `EventDispatchDoc` in the same
818	  file (`{ id, eventId, handlerType, handlerName, targetRole,
819	  noticeType, startedAt, endedAt, status, error }`), index set
820	  `['eventId', 'status', ['eventId', 'status']]`.
821	- `lattice/pulses` — `PulseDoc` in
822	  [packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts](../../packages/plugins/lattice/src/types.ts)
823	  (`{ id, source, triggerType, writId, title, summary, linkUrl,
824	  context, deliveryState, deliveryError?, createdAt, updatedAt }`),
825	  index set `['triggerType', 'source', 'createdAt', 'deliveryState',
826	  'writId', ['deliveryState', 'createdAt'], ['triggerType',
827	  'createdAt']]`.
828	- `animator/sessions` and `animator/transcripts` — `SessionDoc` and
829	  `TranscriptDoc` in `packages/plugins/animator/src/types.ts`, index
830	  sets `['startedAt', 'status', 'conversationId', 'provider']` and
831	  `['sessionId']`.
832	
833	### What Reckonings inherits
834	
835	| From | Lesson |
836	|------|--------|
837	| `clockworks/events` | The `id`-format convention (`<prefix>-<base36_ts>-<hex>` via `generateId`); the append-only stance; the framework-emitter trust convention (the writer stamps `source` / `emitter`, no validation). |
838	| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | The filter-shape-driven index set — only fields that name a real query get an index; compound indexes are added when the query is `WHERE a = … ORDER BY b` shaped. |
839	| `lattice/pulses` | The top-level-fields-plus-typed-context shape (the projection lives at the top so it is filterable, the free-form payload lives in a nested blob); Phase 2 CDC for downstream dispatch (the `book.<owner>.<book>.created` channel petitioners subscribe to is the same shape Lattice consumers use); the lean record body. |
840	| `animator/sessions` | The lean-record / heavy-blob distinction at the apparatus level — even though Reckonings keeps a single book, the framing of "every field is filterable; nothing here is a free-form blob" is what makes the single-book choice work. |
841	
842	### Where Reckonings diverges
843	
844	| From | Divergence | Why |
845	|------|------------|-----|
846	| `clockworks/events` | **No carve-out from CDC auto-wiring.** Events carves itself out because a watcher on the events book observes its own emit. Reckonings has no such recursion. | See CDC Attachment. |
847	| `lattice/pulses` | **No `updatedAt`.** Pulses mutate `deliveryState`; Reckonings has no mutable field. | See Immutability. |
848	| `lattice/pulses` | **No free-form `context: Record<string, unknown>`.** Every Reckonings field is named and indexed-or-filtered-against. | The pulses-style context blob exists because pulse consumers want trigger-keyed structured payloads (e.g. `WritStuckContext`); Reckonings reason metadata is already discriminated by `outcome`, so a separate context blob would be empty. |
849	| `animator/sessions` + `animator/transcripts` | **One book, not two.** Sessions is lean, transcripts is heavy; Reckonings keeps the lean half and has no heavy-blob sibling. | The petition's `intent`, `rationale`, and `context_anchors` live on the petition row, not on the journal. The journal stays at the lean-record scale. |
850	| `clockworks/event_dispatches` | **No per-handler dispatch log.** event_dispatches is a sibling to events recording one row per handler invocation; Reckonings has no per-handler concept. | Petitioner subscribers run as ordinary Clockworks standing orders against the auto-wired CDC event, so the dispatch log they produce already lives in `clockworks/event_dispatches`. A second per-handler log on the Reckonings side would duplicate that. |
851	
852	The dual-table pattern (events + event_dispatches) was considered as a
853	shape for the Reckoner — one table for petitions weighed, a second
854	for "what each petitioner did about it." It was rejected on the same
855	grounds: petitioner reactions are Clockworks standing orders, and the
856	existing `event_dispatches` book already records one row per
857	invocation. Adding a Reckoner-owned dispatch log would re-implement a
858	sibling apparatus's load-bearing data.
859	
860	---
861	
862	## Open Questions
863	
864	The four items this design intentionally leaves for follow-on
865	resolution.
866	
867	- **Petitioner registration.** A parallel commission
868	  (`docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md`) is in flight and
869	  will define how a petitioner declares itself to the Reckoner — the
870	  registration contract, the per-source policy block, the source-id
871	  validator. This Reckonings doc names the auto-wired event surface
872	  registration will plug into (`book.reckoner.reckonings.created`,
873	  with the registered petitioner's standing order filtering on
874	  `outcome` and `source`), but does **not** specify the registration
875	  shape. When the registration commission lands, the per-source
876	  filtering convention should be cross-referenced from here.
877	
878	- **Retention trip-wires.** v0 ships append-only forever with the
879	  storage-growth math above. The trip-wires that warrant a future
880	  retention-design commission are concrete:
881	  1. A Sentinel or Laboratory archival sink lands and an operator
882	     wants to point an archival relay at it.
883	  2. An operator complains about Reckonings book size or about
884	     Stacks query latency on the per-petition timeline query.
885	  3. Six months of production-growth measurement validates the
886	     5.26M rows / year projection (or, more interestingly, refutes
887	     it by 2× or more in either direction).
888	  Until one of those fires, retention is "do nothing." Designing the
889	  archive shape before it has a pull is premature; the future
890	  archival pattern in the Retention section is the placeholder, not
891	  the design.
892	
893	- **Queue-observer fold-back.** The existing
894	  `@shardworks/sentinel-apparatus` is a narrow CDC observer that
895	  emits `reckoner.writ-stuck` / `reckoner.writ-failed` /
896	  `reckoner.queue-drained` Lattice pulses (see
897	  [apparatus/sentinel.md](apparatus/sentinel.md)). It was renamed
898	  out of the `reckoner` plugin id so that id can be reused for the
899	  new petition-scheduling Reckoner. An open question is whether the
900	  queue-observer's responsibilities (writ-lifecycle observation
901	  feeding Lattice pulses) fold back into the new Reckoner core, or
902	  stay in the renamed sibling. The Reckonings book is unaffected either way —
903	  the queue-observer's pulses go to the Lattice, not to the
904	  Reckonings book — but the conceptual seam between "petition
905	  evaluation" and "writ-lifecycle observation" wants to be settled
906	  before the new Reckoner core ships.
907	
908	- **When named events earn their keep.** D8 rejects a named-events
909	  emission surface (`reckoning.accepted`, `reckoning.deferred`, etc.)
910	  on the grounds that auto-wiring + a one-line in-relay outcome
911	  filter serves every consumer auto-wiring can serve. The conditions
912	  under which named events earn their keep:
913	  1. A non-Clockworks consumer of the Reckonings stream appears —
914	     e.g. an external webhook bus, a Discord channel, an HTTP
915	     subscriber — that cannot conveniently consume the framework's
916	     `ChangeEvent<T>` shape and would benefit from a named event
917	     with a flat payload.
918	  2. A measurable performance penalty appears in the in-relay filter
919	     path. Today the filter is one branch on a tagged union; if a
920	     future profiling pass shows the change-event payload is large
921	     enough that all-relays-receive-all-events-and-filter is
922	     measurably worse than per-outcome dispatch, the surface
923	     justifies its own emit path.
924	  3. The validator's reserved-namespace policy gains teeth on
925	     `book.*` events and the same pass extends teeth to `reckoning.*`.
926	  Until at least one of those fires, the auto-wired single channel
927	  remains sufficient.
928	
929	---
930	
931	## See Also
932	
933	- [docs/architecture/clockworks.md](clockworks.md) — the CDC
934	  auto-wiring contract and the reserved-namespace policy.
935	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/lattice.md](apparatus/lattice.md) —
936	  the closest schema precedent for a Reckonings record.
937	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/animator.md](apparatus/animator.md)
938	  — the lean-record / heavy-blob book split (the alternative
939	  considered and rejected here).
940	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md](apparatus/reckoner.md)
941	  — the new petition-scheduling Reckoner whose contract surface
942	  this book layers on top of.
943	- [docs/architecture/apparatus/sentinel.md](apparatus/sentinel.md)
944	  — the renamed queue-observer apparatus that previously held the
945	  `reckoner` plugin id and continues to emit
946	  `reckoner.writ-stuck` / `reckoner.writ-failed` /
947	  `reckoner.queue-drained` Lattice pulses.
948	- [docs/reference/event-catalog.md](../reference/event-catalog.md)
949	  — CDC event names, reserved namespaces, validator policy.
